About 300 words — and, far more importantly, about 90 roots, each of which is now generating four to ten words for you automatically.

ع – ل – مten words from one root
عَلِمَ · عِلْم · عَالِم · عُلَمَاء · مَعْلُوم · عَلَّمَ · مُعَلِّم · تَعَلَّمَ · تَعْلِيم · الْعَلِيم
ش – ه – دfrom Chapter 19
شَهِدَ · شَهَادَة · شَاهِد · شَهِيد · مَشْهُود · أَشْهَدُ
ح – م – دand the names it makes
حَمِدَ · حَمْد · حَامِد · مَحْمُود · مُحَمَّد · أَحْمَد
The honest arithmetic

300 words gets you roughly 50% coverage of running speech. Comfortable comprehension needs about 95%. That gap is not another 300 words — it is another 2,500.

This is not discouraging; it is clarifying. The grammar took three weeks because grammar is finite. Vocabulary will take six to nine months because vocabulary is large. But it is not hard. Twenty new words a day, every day, in a spaced-repetition system, and you arrive.

The wall you were afraid of has already been climbed. What is left is a road, and roads only require walking.

Section 7What the Last Nine Chapters Will Do

ChRegisterWhat changes
22The Qur'anWord order that breaks the rules on purpose. Ellipsis. The absolute object.
23HadithThe isnād as a grammatical object. حَدَّثَنَا, عَنْ, and the technical formulae.
24The lectureFillers, discourse markers, and how a shaykh signposts an argument.
25Fiqh & tafsīrThe technical vocabulary — and how to derive it rather than memorise it.
26–29Real textsUnedited pages, parsed. A real lecture transcript, decoded.
30The road outExactly what to do on Day 31, and for the year after it.
Three weeks in

The shaykh said the thing that matters, so let it stand as the closing line of the first half of your course:

Do not wait until you understand. Listen in order to understand.

End of Chapter 21Next → The Register of the Qur'an
Day 22 · Register: the Qur'an

The Register
of the Qur'an

The grammar is finished. What changes now is how it is used. And Qur'anic Arabic uses it in ways that look, at first, like violations — words in the wrong place, sentences with pieces missing, questions that are not questions. None of it is irregular. All of it is deliberate.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Recognise fronting (التَّقْدِيم) — a word moved out of position, and what that movement means.
  2. Recognise ellipsis (الحَذْف) — a word deliberately deleted, and why the silence is louder than the word.
  3. Learn the absolute object (الْمَفْعُول الْمُطْلَق) — a verb intensified by its own maṣdar.
  4. Stop mistranslating the rhetorical question, which in the Qur'an is almost never a question.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech. Teal is anything in rafʿ — the doer, the topic, the thing said about it. Blue is anything in naṣb — the object, and every "extra": how, when, why, how much. Purple is jarr — after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it. Red is the verb; grey is a particle, which is frozen and has no case at all.

What this chapter is not

This is not tafsīr. It is not theology. It is a chapter about syntax under pressure — what happens to Arabic grammar when a text is trying to say something with maximum force in minimum words.

Every device here also appears in classical poetry and in high oratory. The Qur'an did not invent them. It uses them at a density nothing else reaches.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01تَلَا
he recited; he followed
verb · defectiveت – ل – وmaṣdar تِلَاوَة
يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِهِ.

He recites His verses to them.

02تَدَبَّرَ
he reflected deeply, he pondered
verb · Form Vد – ب – ر
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ؟

Do they not reflect upon the Qur'an?

From دُبُر, the back of a thing. Tadabbur is turning a verse over and looking at what stands behind it.

03رَتَّلَ
he recited slowly and distinctly
verb · Form IIر – ت – لmaṣdar تَرْتِيل
وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا.

And recite the Qur'an with measured recitation.

04الْآيَةُ
the verse; a sign
noun · fem.plural آيَات
وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّمَاوَاتِ.

And among His signs is the creation of the heavens.

One word, two meanings — and they are the same meaning. A verse of the Qur'an and a sign in creation are both آيَة: something that points beyond itself.

05السُّورَةُ
the chapter (of the Qur'an)
noun · fem.plural سُوَر
سُورَةُ الْفَاتِحَةِ.

The Opening Chapter.

06التَّقْدِيمُ
fronting, putting first
rhetorical termق – د – م
التَّقْدِيمُ يُفِيدُ الْحَصْرَ.

Fronting conveys exclusivity.

07الحَذْفُ
deletion, ellipsis
rhetorical termح – ذ – ف
فِي الحَذْفِ بَلَاغَةٌ.

In deletion there is eloquence.

08الْمَفْعُولُ الْمُطْلَقُ
the absolute object
grammar termalways naṣb
كَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا.

And Allah spoke to Mūsā — truly speaking.

09الرَّيْبُ
doubt
nounر – ي – ب
ذٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ.

That is the Book — no doubt is in it.

10الْبَلَاغَةُ
eloquence, rhetorical excellence
nounب – ل – غ
عِلْمُ الْبَلَاغَةِ.

The science of eloquence.

The root means to reach, to arrive. Eloquence, in Arabic, is speech that arrives — that reaches its target exactly.

11أَحَد
one, unique; anyone
noun
قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ.

Say: He is Allah, One.

Distinguish it from وَاحِد, which is one in number. أَحَد is one in a way nothing else is one — uniqueness, not arithmetic.

12الضُّحَىٰ
the morning brightness, the forenoon
noun
وَالضُّحَىٰ. وَاللَّيْلِ إِذَا سَجَىٰ.

By the morning brightness, and by the night when it settles.

13الْقَدْرُ
decree, measure, worth
nounق – د – ر
إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ.

Indeed, We sent it down on the Night of Decree.

Same root as قَدَرَ (to be able) from Day 7, and الْقَدِير. Power, measure and decree are one root — because to decree a thing is to measure out its power.

14آلَاء
favours, bounties
noun · plural
فَبِأَيِّ آلَاءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ؟

So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?

15كَذَّبَ
he denied, he called (something) a lie
verb · Form IIك – ذ – ب
تُكَذِّبَانِ.

You two deny.

Form II of كَذَبَ (to lie). The causative: to declare something a lie. Not the same as lying oneself.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
وَ الضُّحَىٰ.

By the morning brightness.

Sūrat al-Ḍuḥā. Oath at the head; the answer arrives two verses later.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَoath · وَاو القَسَم— الضُّحَىٰthe one sworn by · مُقْسَم بِهِمَجْرُور
02
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ.

Do they not then reflect upon the Qur'an?

A rhetorical question that is really a command. The أَ is not asking; it is rebuking. Qur'anic Arabic uses this constantly, and it is one of the easiest things for a learner to mistranslate.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَفَلَاinterrogative + negation · هَمْزَة الِاسْتِفْهَام— يَتَدَبَّرُونَForm V, present · فِعْل مُضَارِع— الْقُرْآنَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب
03
وَ رَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا.

And recite the Qur'an with measured recitation.

The absolute object — الْمَفْعُول الْمُطْلَق. A verb followed by its own maṣdar in naṣb, purely to intensify it. English has no equivalent; the closest is “recite it, truly reciting it.”

Iʿrāb — word by word وَand · حَرْف عَطْف— رَتِّلِcommand, Form II · فِعْل أَمْرمَجْزُوم الْقُرْآنَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب تَرْتِيلًاits own maṣdar, intensifying the verb · مَفْعُول مُطْلَقمَنْصُوب
04
إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ.

Indeed, We sent it down on the Night of Decree.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّاإِنَّ + نَا · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— أَنْزَلْنَاهُForm IV + object pronoun · فِعْل مَاضٍ— فِيpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— لَيْلَةِafter the preposition, and possessing the next · مُضَاف · مَجْرُورمَجْرُور الْقَدْرِpossessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
05
يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِهِ.

He recites His verses to them.

Watch the feminine plural. آيَاتِهِ is the object, so it should take a fatḥa — but sound feminine plurals take a kasra in naṣb. Chapter 11's exception, and the Qur'an is full of it.

Iʿrāb — word by word يَتْلُوdefective verb, present · فِعْل مُضَارِع— عَلَيْهِمْpreposition + pronoun · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور آيَاتِهِthe object — feminine plural, so kasra · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب
06
ذٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ.

That is the Book — no doubt is in it.

لَا رَيْبَ — the same construction as لَا إِلٰهَ. Not “there is no doubt”; “there is no such thing as doubt in it.” Absolute, categorical.

Iʿrāb — word by word ذٰلِكَthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع الْكِتَابُits substitute · بَدَلمَرْفُوع لَاnegation of the genus · لَا النَّافِيَة— رَيْبَits noun — bare fatḥa · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب فِيهِpreposition + pronoun · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور
07
قُلْ هُوَ اللهُ أَحَدٌ.

Say: He is Allah, One.

Three words in rafʿ in a row — and the whole of tawḥīd inside them. قُلْ is the command of قَالَ, whose word sheet you met on Day 1.

Iʿrāb — word by word قُلْcommand, hollow · فِعْل أَمْرمَجْزُوم هُوَthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع اللهُits substitute · بَدَلمَرْفُوع أَحَدٌthe predicate · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
08
وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ.

And We sent you not except as a mercy to the worlds.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَمَاnegation · حَرْف نَفْي— أَرْسَلْنَاكَForm IV + object pronoun · فِعْل مَاضٍ— إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— رَحْمَةًpurpose · مَفْعُول لِأَجْلِهِمَنْصُوب لِلْعَالَمِينَpreposition + jarr · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور
09
إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ.

You alone we worship.

Fronting is the emphasis. The neutral form would be نَعْبُدُكَ. By dragging the object to the very front, the verse adds a word English needs but Arabic does not: alone.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِيَّاكَthe object, fronted for exclusivity · مَفْعُول بِهِ مُقَدَّممَنْصُوب نَعْبُدُpresent, 1st plural · فِعْل مُضَارِع—
10
فَبِأَيِّ آلَاءِ رَبِّكُمَا تُكَذِّبَانِ.

So which of the favours of your Lord will you two deny?

The refrain of Sūrat al-Raḥmān, and the only place most learners ever meet the dual verb. تُكَذِّبَانِ — the أَنْتُمَا row of a Form II grid, addressed to mankind and jinn.

Iʿrāb — word by word فَبِأَيِّفَـ + بِـ + أَيّ · حَرْف · اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَام— آلَاءِpossessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور رَبِّكُمَاpossessed by the previous · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور تُكَذِّبَانِForm II, dual, present · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — Three Devices of Qur'anic Syntax

One: fronting — التَّقْدِيم

Arabic word order is flexible because the case endings carry the roles. That flexibility is not idle. When a word is moved out of its neutral position, the movement means something: it foregrounds, restricts, or emphasises.

NeutralFrontedWhat the fronting adds
نَعْبُدُكَ
we worship You		إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُYou alone — exclusivity
يُسْرٌ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ
ease is with hardship		إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًاthe inseparability — with the hardship itself
—لِلهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِTo Allah belongs — and to no other
The rule the rhetoricians state

التَّقْدِيمُ يُفِيدُ الْحَصْرَ — "fronting conveys exclusivity."

So when you hear a prepositional phrase or an object arriving before the verb, do not simply reorder it into English and move on. Ask: what is being excluded? The answer is almost always the point of the verse.

Two: ellipsis — الحَذْف

Arabic will delete a word it considers obvious — and the deletion is a rhetorical act, not an economy.

You have already met the most famous case. لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ is missing its predicate. The full form would be لَا إِلٰهَ حَقٌّ إِلَّا اللهُ — "there is no god in truth but Allah."

Why delete it? Because a deleted word is supplied by the listener, and a thought the listener completes himself lands harder than one he is handed. The silence does work that a word could not.

وَاللهُ يَدْعُو إِلَىٰ دَارِ السَّلَامِ

And Allah calls to the Abode of Peace — and calls whom? The object is deleted, and so the invitation is universal.

Three: the absolute object — الْمَفْعُول الْمُطْلَق

A verb, followed by its own maṣdar, in naṣb. It has no English equivalent whatsoever.

رَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا

Recite the Qur'an — with a reciting.

Why say it twice? Because the maṣdar intensifies the verb. Roughly: "recite it, and truly recite it." And when the maṣdar carries an adjective, the intensification becomes specific:

كَلَّمَ اللهُ مُوسَىٰ تَكْلِيمًا

And Allah spoke to Mūsā — truly speaking.

Theologically, that final word is doing enormous work: it forecloses any reading of "spoke" as metaphor. The absolute object exists precisely to say and I mean this literally.

And the question that is not a question

Qur'anic Arabic opens sentences with أَ or أَفَلَا constantly, and almost never to request information:

ArabicLooks likeActually means
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ؟"Do they not reflect?"a rebuke — they ought to reflect!
أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ؟"Did We not expand your breast?"a reminder — We certainly did.
هَلْ جَزَاءُ الْإِحْسَانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسَانُ؟"Is the reward of good anything but good?"an assertion — it is nothing else.

The technical name is الِاسْتِفْهَام التَّقْرِيرِيّ — the confirming interrogative. It asks in order to force the listener to answer, silently, yes. Translate it as a question and you have flattened it into a query. Hear it as a rebuke or an affirmation, and you have heard the verse.

Section 5Reading Practice — Sūrat al-Fātiḥah, Parsed

بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ

الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ. الرَّحْمٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ. مَالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ.

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ.

اِهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ. صِرَاطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ، غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ.

PhraseWhat is happeningChapter
بِسْمِ اللهِpreposition + iḍāfa. Everything after بِـ is in jarr.4
الْحَمْدُ لِلهِnominal sentence; predicate is a prepositional phrase.1
رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَiḍāfa in apposition — بَدَل, so it copies the jarr of اللهِ.4
مَالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِa nested iḍāfa, three deep. Everything after the first is in jarr.4
إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُfronting — and therefore exclusivity. You alone.22
نَسْتَعِينُForm X — “we seek help.” The seeking mould.10
اِهْدِنَاdefective verb, command → final letter deleted, then a pronoun attached.13
الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَobject in naṣb, and its adjective following it into naṣb.5
الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْrelative clause with its returner pronoun — عَلَيْهِمْ.8
غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِغَيْر as exception, in jarr, followed by a passive done-to noun.19 · 17
Look at what you just did

You have parsed the Fātiḥah completely. Every word. Every ending. Every device.

And notice the passive in the final verse: الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ — “those upon whom wrath has fallen.” The verse does not say upon whom You are angry. The agent is deliberately deleted — the same reverent ellipsis you studied on Day 17 — while the very next phrase, أَنْعَمْتَ, names the agent explicitly: You favoured them.

Favour is attributed directly to Allah. Wrath is left agentless. That is not an accident of style. That is the entire theology of the sūrah, expressed through the choice of voice.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

قَالَ اللهُ: «إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ». وَلَمْ يَقُلْ: «نَعْبُدُكَ». فَمَا الْفَرْقُ؟

الْفَرْقُ كُلُّ شَيْءٍ. «نَعْبُدُكَ» تَعْنِي: نَحْنُ نَعْبُدُكَ. وَقَدْ نَعْبُدُ غَيْرَكَ أَيْضًا!

أَمَّا «إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ» فَتَعْنِي: إِيَّاكَ وَحْدَكَ. لَا أَحَدَ سِوَاكَ.

وَكَيْفَ عَرَفْنَا؟ مِنَ التَّقْدِيمِ. قَدَّمَ الْمَفْعُولَ، فَأَفَادَ الْحَصْرَ.

فَانْظُرُوا: كَلِمَةٌ وَاحِدَةٌ تَحَرَّكَتْ مِنْ مَكَانِهَا — وَتَغَيَّرَ الدِّينُ كُلُّهُ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، وَهَلْ يَعْرِفُ الْعَرَبِيُّ هٰذَا بِلَا دِرَاسَةٍ؟

الشَّيْخُ:

يَشْعُرُ بِهِ، وَلَا يُفَسِّرُهُ. وَأَنْتَ الْآنَ تَشْعُرُ بِهِ وَتُفَسِّرُهُ. فَأَيُّكُمَا أَفْضَلُ؟

Shaykh: Allah said: Iyyāka naʿbud. And He did not say: naʿbuduka. So what is the difference? The difference is everything. Naʿbuduka means: we worship You. And we might worship others too! But iyyāka naʿbud means: You alone. No one besides You. And how did we know that? From the fronting. He put the object first — and so it conveyed exclusivity. So look: one word moved from its place — and the whole religion changed.

Student: Shaykh, does a native Arab know this without studying?

Shaykh: He feels it, and cannot explain it. And you now feel it and explain it. So which of you two is better off?

New: سِوَاكَ besides You · قَدَّمَ he put first (II) · أَفَادَ it conveyed (IV) · تَحَرَّكَتْ it moved (V) · تَغَيَّرَ it changed (V)

Say it yourself

Three lines of the register itself — say them as recitation, not as reading.

الْحَمْدُ لِلّٰهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ.

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ.

رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْمًا.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb تَلَا
maṣdar تِلَاوَة recitation
to recite, to follow
Form I · defective · يَتْلُو
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIتَلَوْتُأَتْلُو——
نَحْنُweتَلَوْنَانَتْلُو——
أَنْتَyou (m.)تَلَوْتَتَتْلُواُتْلُلَا تَتْلُ
أَنْتِyou (f.)تَلَوْتِتَتْلِينَاُتْلِيلَا تَتْلِي
هُوَheتَلَايَتْلُو——
هِيَsheتَلَتْتَتْلُو——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)تَلَوْتُمَاتَتْلُوَانِاُتْلُوَالَا تَتْلُوَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)تَلَوْتُمْتَتْلُونَاُتْلُوالَا تَتْلُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)تَلَوْتُمَاتَتْلُوَانِاُتْلُوَالَا تَتْلُوَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)تَلَوْتُنَّتَتْلُونَاُتْلُونَلَا تَتْلُونَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)تَلَوَايَتْلُوَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)تَلَوْايَتْلُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)تَلَتَاتَتْلُوَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)تَلَوْنَيَتْلُونَ——

The Qur'an's own word for reciting itself: يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِهِ. Note the root also means to follow — one word follows another off the tongue, and a recitation is a chain of followings. Defective, so the command drops its final letter: اُتْلُ.

verb تَدَبَّرَ
maṣdar تَدَبُّر reflection
to reflect deeply, to ponder
Form V · يَتَدَبَّرُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIتَدَبَّرْتُأَتَدَبَّرُ——
نَحْنُweتَدَبَّرْنَانَتَدَبَّرُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)تَدَبَّرْتَتَتَدَبَّرُتَدَبَّرْلَا تَتَدَبَّرْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)تَدَبَّرْتِتَتَدَبَّرِينَتَدَبَّرِيلَا تَتَدَبَّرِي
هُوَheتَدَبَّرَيَتَدَبَّرُ——
هِيَsheتَدَبَّرَتْتَتَدَبَّرُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)تَدَبَّرْتُمَاتَتَدَبَّرَانِتَدَبَّرَالَا تَتَدَبَّرَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)تَدَبَّرْتُمْتَتَدَبَّرُونَتَدَبَّرُوالَا تَتَدَبَّرُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)تَدَبَّرْتُمَاتَتَدَبَّرَانِتَدَبَّرَالَا تَتَدَبَّرَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)تَدَبَّرْتُنَّتَتَدَبَّرْنَتَدَبَّرْنَلَا تَتَدَبَّرْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)تَدَبَّرَايَتَدَبَّرَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)تَدَبَّرُوايَتَدَبَّرُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)تَدَبَّرَتَاتَتَدَبَّرَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)تَدَبَّرْنَيَتَدَبَّرْنَ——

From دُبُر — the back, the far end of a thing. To do tadabbur is to turn a verse over and look at what is behind it. The Qur'an commands it directly: أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ.

verb رَتَّلَ
maṣdar تَرْتِيل measured recitation
to recite slowly and distinctly
Form II · يُرَتِّلُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIرَتَّلْتُأُرَتِّلُ——
نَحْنُweرَتَّلْنَانُرَتِّلُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)رَتَّلْتَتُرَتِّلُرَتِّلْلَا تُرَتِّلْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)رَتَّلْتِتُرَتِّلِينَرَتِّلِيلَا تُرَتِّلِي
هُوَheرَتَّلَيُرَتِّلُ——
هِيَsheرَتَّلَتْتُرَتِّلُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)رَتَّلْتُمَاتُرَتِّلَانِرَتِّلَالَا تُرَتِّلَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)رَتَّلْتُمْتُرَتِّلُونَرَتِّلُوالَا تُرَتِّلُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)رَتَّلْتُمَاتُرَتِّلَانِرَتِّلَالَا تُرَتِّلَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)رَتَّلْتُنَّتُرَتِّلْنَرَتِّلْنَلَا تُرَتِّلْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)رَتَّلَايُرَتِّلَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)رَتَّلُوايُرَتِّلُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)رَتَّلَتَاتُرَتِّلَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)رَتَّلْنَيُرَتِّلْنَ——

وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا — “and recite the Qur'an with measured recitation.” Note the construction: the verb, then its own maṣdar in naṣb. Arabic calls this the absolute object — a maṣdar used to intensify its own verb, and there is no English equivalent at all.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to reciteتَلَايَتْلُوتِلَاوَةتَالٍتُلِيَيُتْلَىٰمَتْلُوّاُتْلُ
to reflect (V)تَدَبَّرَيَتَدَبَّرُتَدَبُّرمُتَدَبِّرتُدُبِّرَيُتَدَبَّرُمُتَدَبَّرتَدَبَّرْ
to recite slowly (II)رَتَّلَيُرَتِّلُتَرْتِيلمُرَتِّلرُتِّلَيُرَتَّلُمُرَتَّلرَتِّلْ
One root through all eight moulds — تَلَا

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ت ل و, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. تَلَا الْإِمَامُ آيَاتٍ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ. — The imam recited verses from the Qur’an.

المضارع · present active. يَتْلُو الْقَارِئُ كِتَابَ اللهِ بِتَرْتِيلٍ. — The reciter recites the Book of Allah with measured tone.

المصدر · the action itself. تِلَاوَةُ الْقُرْآنِ عِبَادَةٌ. — Reciting the Qur’an is an act of worship.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. التَّالِي لِكِتَابِ اللهِ مَأْجُورٌ. — The one who recites the Book of Allah is rewarded.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. تُلِيَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْآيَاتُ فَبَكَوْا. — The verses were recited to them, and they wept.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. يُتْلَىٰ الْقُرْآنُ فِي كُلِّ بَيْتٍ. — The Qur’an is recited in every home.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. الْمَتْلُوُّ كَلَامُ اللهِ. — What is recited is the speech of Allah.

الأمر · command. اُتْلُ مَا أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ. — Recite what has been revealed to you.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

تَلَاhe recitedتَدَبَّرَhe reflected (V) رَتَّلَhe recited slowly (II)الْآيَةُverse; sign السُّورَةُthe chapterالتَّقْدِيمُfronting الحَذْفُellipsisالْمَفْعُولُ الْمُطْلَقُabsolute object الرَّيْبُdoubtالْبَلَاغَةُeloquence أَحَدone, uniqueالضُّحَىٰmorning brightness الْقَدْرُdecree, measureآلَاءfavours كَذَّبَhe denied (II)

Spiral recap — Chapters 13, 17 & 19

دَعَاhe supplicatedهَدَىٰhe guided الصِّرَاطُthe pathبَنَىٰhe built قِيلَit was saidإِلَّاexcept إِنَّمَاonlyالشَّهَادَةُtestimony
Before you close the book

The shaykh's question was not rhetorical. A native speaker feels that إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ means You alone, without knowing why. You now feel it and know why.

That is not a lesser kind of understanding. In some ways it is a greater one — and it is available to anyone willing to spend three weeks on the grammar.

End of Chapter 22Next → The Register of Hadith
Day 23 · Register: hadith

The Chain,
and the Text

A hadith is two things bolted together: a chain of names, and a text. The chain is almost pure grammar — prepositions, iḍāfas, and a handful of technical verbs. Learn it once and you can skip it at speed, or read it closely, exactly as a scholar does.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Parse an isnād — the chain of narration — and understand its grammar completely.
  2. Distinguish the technical transmission verbs: حَدَّثَنَا, أَخْبَرَنَا, عَنْ — which are not interchangeable.
  3. Read the grading vocabulary: صَحِيح, حَسَن, ضَعِيف, مُتَّفَقٌ عَلَيْهِ.
  4. Meet the diptote — a class of nouns that refuses to take a kasra, and which every proper name in an isnād belongs to.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech. Teal is anything in rafʿ — the doer, the topic, the thing said about it. Blue is anything in naṣb — the object, and every "extra": how, when, why, how much. Purple is jarr — after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it. Red is the verb; grey is a particle, which is frozen and has no case at all.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01رَوَىٰ
he narrated, he transmitted
verb · defectiveر – و – يmaṣdar رِوَايَة
رَوَاهُ الْبُخَارِيُّ وَمُسْلِمٌ.

Al-Bukhārī and Muslim narrated it.

02حَدَّثَنَا
he narrated to us
verb · Form IIthe highest grade of transmission
حَدَّثَنَا مُحَمَّدُ بْنُ بَشَّارٍ.

Muḥammad ibn Bashshār narrated to us.

This is technical, not casual. It means the narrator heard the hadith directly, orally, from his teacher's mouth, in a session. Any weaker mode of transmission gets a different verb — and the hadith scholars will notice.

03أَخْبَرَنَا
he informed us
verb · Form IVa slightly lower grade
أَخْبَرَنَا عَبْدُ اللهِ.

ʿAbdullāh informed us.

Traditionally used when the student read the text back to the teacher, rather than hearing it from him. A finer distinction than any European scholarly tradition ever made.

04عَنْ
on the authority of
prepositionthe weakest link
عَنْ أَبِي هُرَيْرَةَ.

On the authority of Abū Hurayrah.

And here is why the hadith scholars cared: عَنْ does not say the narrator heard it directly. It leaves the mode of transmission unstated — which is precisely the gap a forger would exploit. An entire science, عِلْم الْعِلَل, exists to police it.

05الْإِسْنَادُ
the chain of narration
noun · maṣdar of IVس – ن – د
الْإِسْنَادُ مِنَ الدِّينِ.

The chain of narration is part of the religion.

The root means to lean a thing against a support. A hadith is propped up on its chain — and if the prop is weak, the text falls.

06الْمَتْنُ
the text (of a hadith)
nounthe opposite of إِسْنَاد
صَحَّ الْإِسْنَادُ وَالْمَتْنُ.

Both the chain and the text are sound.

07الرَّاوِي
the narrator
doer-nounplural الرُّوَاة
الرُّوَاةُ ثِقَاتٌ.

The narrators are trustworthy.

08ثِقَة
trustworthy, reliable
noun / adjectiveplural ثِقَات
هٰذَا رَاوٍ ثِقَةٌ.

This is a trustworthy narrator.

09صَحِيح — حَسَن — ضَعِيف
authentic — good — weak
the three gradingsall فَعِيل mould
حَدِيثٌ صَحِيحٌ. حَدِيثٌ حَسَنٌ. حَدِيثٌ ضَعِيفٌ.

An authentic hadith. A good hadith. A weak hadith.

10مُتَّفَقٌ عَلَيْهِ
agreed upon (by Bukhārī and Muslim)
done-to noun of VIIIthe highest verdict
مُتَّفَقٌ عَلَيْهِ.

Agreed upon.

11النَّصِيحَةُ
sincere counsel, sincerity
nounن – ص – ح
الدِّينُ النَّصِيحَةُ.

The religion is sincere counsel.

Notoriously hard to translate. The root means to purify honey of its wax — so نَصِيحَة is advice with nothing self-serving mixed into it.

12الضَّرَرُ
harm
nounض – ر – ر
لَا ضَرَرَ وَلَا ضِرَارَ.

There is no harm and no reciprocating harm.

13كَذَبَ
he lied
verb · soundك – ذ – ب
مَنْ كَذَبَ عَلَيَّ مُتَعَمِّدًا.

Whoever lies about me deliberately...

14مُتَعَمِّد
deliberate, intentional
doer of Vع – م – د
كَذَبَ عَلَيَّ مُتَعَمِّدًا.

He lied about me deliberately.

Always a ḥāl, always in naṣb — and in this hadith the whole legal weight rests on it. An honest error is not the crime; deliberateness is.

15بْن
son of
nounthe joint of every isnād
مُحَمَّدُ بْنُ إِسْمَاعِيلَ.

Muḥammad, son of Ismāʿīl.

Every بْن opens a new iḍāfa. A long chain of names is, grammatically, a long chain of possessions — which is why the endings cascade the way they do.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
حَدَّثَنَا أَبُو بَكْرٍ.

Abū Bakr narrated to us.

The highest grade of transmission. حَدَّثَنَا means the narrator heard it directly from his teacher. Note أَبُو — a five-noun, showing rafʿ with a letter, not a vowel (the masterclass, Section 8).

Iʿrāb — word by word حَدَّثَنَاForm II + object pronoun · فِعْل مَاضٍ— أَبُوthe doer — a “five noun,” shown by a letter · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع بَكْرٍpossessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
02
عَنْ أَبِي هُرَيْرَةَ.

On the authority of Abū Hurayrah.

Two irregularities in three words. أَبِي shows jarr with a ي. And هُرَيْرَةَ is a diptote — a class of nouns that cannot take a kasra, and use a fatḥa for jarr instead. Proper names ending in ة nearly always behave this way.

Iʿrāb — word by word عَنْpreposition of the isnād · حَرْف جَرّ— أَبِيafter the preposition — again a five-noun · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور هُرَيْرَةَpossessed — a diptote, so fatḥa for jarr · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
03
قَالَ رَسُولُ اللهِ.

The Messenger of Allah ﷺ said.

Iʿrāb — word by word قَالَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— رَسُولُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع اللهِpossessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
04
رَوَاهُ الْبُخَارِيُّ.

Al-Bukhārī narrated it.

The closing formula of a hadith citation. Note the ـهُ — the “returner” pronoun pointing back at the hadith just quoted.

Iʿrāb — word by word رَوَاهُdefective verb + object pronoun · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الْبُخَارِيُّthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع
05
مُتَّفَقٌ عَلَيْهِ.

Agreed upon.

The most weighty verdict in hadith. It means: both Bukhārī and Muslim narrated it. Grammatically it is the done-to noun of Form VIII اِتَّفَقَ — “to agree.”

Iʿrāb — word by word مُتَّفَقٌthe predicate of a deleted topic · خَبَرمَرْفُوع عَلَيْهِpreposition + pronoun · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور
06
صَحِيحٌ.

Authentic.

The grading. Three you must know: صَحِيح (authentic) · حَسَن (good) · ضَعِيف (weak). All three are فَعِيل-mould adjectives — Chapter 5.

Iʿrāb — word by word صَحِيحٌthe predicate · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
07
إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ.

Actions are only by intentions.

The first hadith in Bukhārī, and the first in al-Nawawī's Forty. If you memorise one hadith in Arabic, memorise this.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّمَاrestriction · أَدَاة حَصْر— الْأَعْمَالُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع بِالنِّيَّاتِpreposition + jarr — the predicate · خَبَر · شِبْه جُمْلَةمَجْرُور
08
مَنْ كَذَبَ عَلَيَّ مُتَعَمِّدًا.

Whoever lies about me deliberately...

A ḥāl (Chapter 18) inside a conditional (Chapter 15). And the ḥāl is doing all the legal work: an accidental error is not the same crime as a deliberate one, and the whole distinction rides on one fatḥa.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَنْconditional — “whoever” · اِسْم شَرْط— كَذَبَverb of the condition · فِعْل الشَّرْط— عَلَيَّpreposition + pronoun · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور مُتَعَمِّدًاthe circumstance · حَالمَنْصُوب
09
الدِّينُ النَّصِيحَةُ.

The religion is sincere counsel.

Two words, and a whole hadith. A nominal sentence, both halves definite — so strictly it needs a pronoun of separation; the Prophet ﷺ omits it, and the omission gives the sentence the force of an equation: religion is naṣīḥah, exactly and exhaustively.

Iʿrāb — word by word الدِّينُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع النَّصِيحَةُthe predicate · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
10
لَا ضَرَرَ وَلَا ضِرَارَ.

There is no harm and no reciprocating of harm.

A legal maxim, and one of the pillars of Islamic jurisprudence. Two لَاs of the genus, two bare fatḥas — meaning the prohibition is absolute, not partial.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاnegation of the genus · لَا النَّافِيَة— ضَرَرَits noun · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب وَلَاand no · — ضِرَارَits noun · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب

Section 4Grammar — The Isnād, and the Diptote

The chain is a grammatical machine

Look at a full isnād and notice how little is happening:

حَدَّثَنَا أَبُو بَكْرٍ، حَدَّثَنَا سُفْيَانُ، عَنْ مَنْصُورٍ، عَنْ أَبِي هُرَيْرَةَ، قَالَ:

Abū Bakr narrated to us; Sufyān narrated to us; on the authority of Manṣūr; on the authority of Abū Hurayrah, who said:

Three grammatical structures, repeated:

StructureCase
حَدَّثَنَا + a namethe name is the doer → rafʿ
عَنْ + a nameafter a preposition → jarr
فُلَان بْن فُلَانiḍāfa → the second name in jarr

That is the entire grammar of an isnād. Once you see it, a chain of ten names stops being a wall of text and becomes a rhythm you can read at speed — or skip, exactly as scholars do when they want only the text.

The diptote — the noun that refuses the kasra

Now the one genuine surprise. Look again at عَنْ أَبِي هُرَيْرَةَ.

هُرَيْرَةَ is after a preposition. It should be in jarr, and jarr means a kasra. But it has a fatḥa.

This is not an error

A class of nouns called الْمَمْنُوع مِنَ الصَّرْف — the diptote — is forbidden from taking a kasra or a tanwīn. Instead it uses a fatḥa for jarr.

Which nouns? Chiefly these, and they are exactly the nouns an isnād is made of:

  • Proper names ending in ة: هُرَيْرَة · طَلْحَة · مُعَاوِيَة · فَاطِمَة · حَمْزَة
  • Non-Arabic names: إِبْرَاهِيم · إِسْمَاعِيل · يُوسُف · مَرْيَم
  • Comparatives (أَفْعَل): أَكْبَر · أَفْضَل
  • Certain broken plurals: مَسَاجِد · مَذَاهِب · أَحَادِيث

So: فِي مَسَاجِدَ, not fī masājidin. مِنْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ, not min Ibrāhīma-in. And عَنْ أَبِي هُرَيْرَةَ, with a fatḥa where you expected a kasra.

Why this matters for your ear

Until now, your rule has been: kasra after a preposition. From today it has an exception, and the exception is common — it covers most proper names and many plurals.

So when you hear a fatḥa where a kasra should be, do not assume you misheard. Ask: is this a diptote? Nine times out of ten in an isnād, it is.

Reading the grading

VerdictMeaningWhat it is, grammatically
صَحِيحauthenticفَعِيل adjective — “sound”
حَسَنgood — sound, but slightly less strongadjective
ضَعِيفweak — not to be relied uponفَعِيل adjective
مَوْضُوعfabricated — a forgerydone-to noun: “placed” there by someone
مُتَّفَقٌ عَلَيْهِagreed upon by Bukhārī and Muslimdone-to noun of Form VIII

Note مَوْضُوع — “fabricated” — is the done-to noun of وَضَعَ, to place. A forged hadith is one that was put there by a human hand. The passive is doing the accusing.

Section 5Reading Practice — A Full Hadith, Unedited

حَدَّثَنَا الْحُمَيْدِيُّ، قَالَ: حَدَّثَنَا سُفْيَانُ، قَالَ: حَدَّثَنَا يَحْيَى بْنُ سَعِيدٍ الْأَنْصَارِيُّ، قَالَ: أَخْبَرَنِي مُحَمَّدُ بْنُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ التَّيْمِيُّ، أَنَّهُ سَمِعَ عَلْقَمَةَ بْنَ وَقَّاصٍ اللَّيْثِيَّ يَقُولُ: سَمِعْتُ عُمَرَ بْنَ الْخَطَّابِ رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُ عَلَى الْمِنْبَرِ قَالَ:

سَمِعْتُ رَسُولَ اللهِ ﷺ يَقُولُ: «إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ، وَإِنَّمَا لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مَا نَوَىٰ».

Al-Ḥumaydī narrated to us; he said: Sufyān narrated to us; he said: Yaḥyā ibn Saʿīd al-Anṣārī narrated to us; he said: Muḥammad ibn Ibrāhīm al-Taymī informed me, that he heard ʿAlqamah ibn Waqqāṣ al-Laythī saying: I heard ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb, may Allah be pleased with him, say on the pulpit:

I heard the Messenger of Allah ﷺ say: "Actions are only by intentions, and for every person is only what he intended."

The very first hadith in Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhārī

Read the chain again and count what you can now identify: حَدَّثَنَا (Form II + pronoun) · أَخْبَرَنِي (Form IV + pronoun) · بْنُ in rafʿ, بْنَ in naṣb — because the name it belongs to changes case · إِبْرَاهِيمَ a diptote, fatḥa for jarr · عَلْقَمَةَ a diptote · عُمَرَ a diptote · أَنَّهُ — أَنَّ + a pronoun · يَقُولُ — a present verb functioning as a ḥāl: heard him while he was saying.

Six chapters of grammar in one paragraph of names — and you can now walk through every ending in it.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

قَالَ ابْنُ الْمُبَارَكِ: «الْإِسْنَادُ مِنَ الدِّينِ. وَلَوْلَا الْإِسْنَادُ، لَقَالَ مَنْ شَاءَ مَا شَاءَ».

وَانْظُرُوا إِلَى «لَوْلَا» — شَرْطٌ يَدُلُّ عَلَى الِامْتِنَاعِ. أَيْ: لَمْ يَقُلْ مَنْ شَاءَ مَا شَاءَ، لِأَنَّ الْإِسْنَادَ مَوْجُودٌ.

وَبَعْضُ الطُّلَّابِ يَمَلُّ مِنَ الْأَسَانِيدِ، وَيَقُولُ: أَعْطِنِي الْمَتْنَ!

فَأَقُولُ: الْمَتْنُ بِلَا إِسْنَادٍ كَلَامٌ. وَالْكَلَامُ يَقُولُهُ كُلُّ أَحَدٍ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، وَلٰكِنَّ الْأَسْمَاءَ كَثِيرَةٌ وَصَعْبَةٌ.

الشَّيْخُ:

لَيْسَتْ صَعْبَةً، بَلْ غَرِيبَةٌ. وَالْغَرِيبُ يَصِيرُ مَأْلُوفًا بِالتَّكْرَارِ.

Shaykh: Ibn al-Mubārak said: "The chain of narration is part of the religion. And were it not for the chain, whoever wished would say whatever he wished." And look at لَوْلَا — a conditional indicating impossibility. That is: no one did say whatever he wished, because the chain exists. And some students grow bored of the chains, and say: give me the text! And I say: a text without a chain is just talk. And talk — anyone can produce.

Student: Shaykh, but the names are many, and difficult.

Shaykh: They are not difficult — they are unfamiliar. And the unfamiliar becomes familiar through repetition.

New: يَدُلُّ it indicates · الِامْتِنَاع impossibility · يَمَلُّ he grows bored · أَعْطِنِي give me · مَأْلُوف familiar · التَّكْرَار repetition

Say it yourself

The vocabulary of transmission, in your own voice.

حَدَّثَنَا الشَّيْخُ حَدِيثًا صَحِيحًا.

قَالَ رَسُولُ اللهِ: إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ.

رَوَى الْبُخَارِيُّ هٰذَا الْحَدِيثَ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb رَوَىٰ
maṣdar رِوَايَة narration
to narrate, to transmit
Form I · defective · يَرْوِي
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIرَوَيْتُأَرْوِي——
نَحْنُweرَوَيْنَانَرْوِي——
أَنْتَyou (m.)رَوَيْتَتَرْوِياِرْوِلَا تَرْوِ
أَنْتِyou (f.)رَوَيْتِتَرْوِينَاِرْوِيلَا تَرْوِي
هُوَheرَوَىٰيَرْوِي——
هِيَsheرَوَتْتَرْوِي——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)رَوَيْتُمَاتَرْوِيَانِاِرْوِيَالَا تَرْوِيَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)رَوَيْتُمْتَرْوُونَاِرْوُوالَا تَرْوُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)رَوَيْتُمَاتَرْوِيَانِاِرْوِيَالَا تَرْوِيَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)رَوَيْتُنَّتَرْوِينَاِرْوِينَلَا تَرْوِينَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)رَوَيَايَرْوِيَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)رَوَوْايَرْوُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)رَوَتَاتَرْوِيَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)رَوَيْنَيَرْوِينَ——

The verb of the entire hadith science. رَوَاهُ الْبُخَارِيُّ — “al-Bukhārī narrated it.” Its doer-noun, رَاوٍ, is a narrator; its maṣdar, رِوَايَة, is a narration. Three words you will meet on every page of a hadith book, all from this one grid.

verb حَدَّثَ
maṣdar تَحْدِيث narrating
to narrate, to relate (to someone)
Form II · يُحَدِّثُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIحَدَّثْتُأُحَدِّثُ——
نَحْنُweحَدَّثْنَانُحَدِّثُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)حَدَّثْتَتُحَدِّثُحَدِّثْلَا تُحَدِّثْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)حَدَّثْتِتُحَدِّثِينَحَدِّثِيلَا تُحَدِّثِي
هُوَheحَدَّثَيُحَدِّثُ——
هِيَsheحَدَّثَتْتُحَدِّثُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)حَدَّثْتُمَاتُحَدِّثَانِحَدِّثَالَا تُحَدِّثَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)حَدَّثْتُمْتُحَدِّثُونَحَدِّثُوالَا تُحَدِّثُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)حَدَّثْتُمَاتُحَدِّثَانِحَدِّثَالَا تُحَدِّثَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)حَدَّثْتُنَّتُحَدِّثْنَحَدِّثْنَلَا تُحَدِّثْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)حَدَّثَايُحَدِّثَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)حَدَّثُوايُحَدِّثُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)حَدَّثَتَاتُحَدِّثَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)حَدَّثْنَيُحَدِّثْنَ——

حَدَّثَنَا — “he narrated to us” — is the first word of thousands of hadiths, and the technical marker of the highest grade of transmission: the student heard it directly from the teacher's mouth. Its doer-noun is مُحَدِّث, a hadith scholar.

verb أَخْبَرَ
maṣdar إِخْبَار informing
to inform, to report
Form IV · يُخْبِرُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIأَخْبَرْتُأُخْبِرُ——
نَحْنُweأَخْبَرْنَانُخْبِرُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)أَخْبَرْتَتُخْبِرُأَخْبِرْلَا تُخْبِرْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)أَخْبَرْتِتُخْبِرِينَأَخْبِرِيلَا تُخْبِرِي
هُوَheأَخْبَرَيُخْبِرُ——
هِيَsheأَخْبَرَتْتُخْبِرُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)أَخْبَرْتُمَاتُخْبِرَانِأَخْبِرَالَا تُخْبِرَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)أَخْبَرْتُمْتُخْبِرُونَأَخْبِرُوالَا تُخْبِرُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)أَخْبَرْتُمَاتُخْبِرَانِأَخْبِرَالَا تُخْبِرَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)أَخْبَرْتُنَّتُخْبِرْنَأَخْبِرْنَلَا تُخْبِرْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)أَخْبَرَايُخْبِرَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)أَخْبَرُوايُخْبِرُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)أَخْبَرَتَاتُخْبِرَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)أَخْبَرْنَيُخْبِرْنَ——

أَخْبَرَنَا — “he informed us” — the second technical formula of transmission, ranking just below حَدَّثَنَا. Related: خَبَر, a report — and the very same word you learned on Day 1 as the predicate of a nominal sentence. Arabic uses one word for “news” and for “what is said about the subject.”

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to narrateرَوَىٰيَرْوِيرِوَايَةرَاوٍرُوِيَيُرْوَىٰمَرْوِيّاِرْوِ
to relate (II)حَدَّثَيُحَدِّثُتَحْدِيثمُحَدِّثحُدِّثَيُحَدَّثُمُحَدَّثحَدِّثْ
to inform (IV)أَخْبَرَيُخْبِرُإِخْبَارمُخْبِرأُخْبِرَيُخْبَرُمُخْبَرأَخْبِرْ
One root through all eight moulds — رَوَىٰ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ر و ي, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. رَوَىٰ الْبُخَارِيُّ هٰذَا الْحَدِيثَ. — Al-Bukhārī narrated this hadith.

المضارع · present active. يَرْوِي الرَّاوِي عَنْ شَيْخِهِ. — The narrator narrates from his teacher.

المصدر · the action itself. لِهٰذَا الْحَدِيثِ رِوَايَاتٌ كَثِيرَةٌ. — This hadith has many narrations.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الرَّاوِي الثِّقَةُ تُقْبَلُ رِوَايَتُهُ. — The trustworthy narrator’s report is accepted.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. رُوِيَ عَنِ النَّبِيِّ ﷺ أَنَّهُ قَالَ. — It was narrated from the Prophet ﷺ that he said.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. يُرْوَىٰ هٰذَا الْخَبَرُ بِأَسَانِيدَ صَحِيحَةٍ. — This report is narrated with sound chains.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. الْحَدِيثُ الْمَرْوِيُّ يُدْرَسُ فِي الْإِسْنَادِ. — The narrated hadith is studied in [the science of] isnād.

الأمر · command. اِرْوِ الْحَدِيثَ كَمَا سَمِعْتَهُ. — Narrate the hadith as you heard it.

The word you have been using since Day 1

خَبَر — a report. And also, since Chapter 1, the predicate of a nominal sentence.

Arabic uses one word for both, and the logic is exact: the khabar is the news about the subject. What you say about الْعِلْمُ is the "news" of it. The grammarians did not borrow a term from journalism. Journalism borrowed from them.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

رَوَىٰhe narratedحَدَّثَنَاhe narrated to us أَخْبَرَنَاhe informed usعَنْon the authority of الْإِسْنَادُthe chainالْمَتْنُthe text الرَّاوِيthe narratorثِقَةtrustworthy صَحِيحauthenticضَعِيفweak مُتَّفَقٌ عَلَيْهِagreed uponالنَّصِيحَةُsincere counsel الضَّرَرُharmمُتَعَمِّدdeliberate بْنson of

Spiral recap — Chapters 4, 14 & 18

أَهْلُpeople ofالِابْنُthe son الْأَبُthe fatherالحَالُthe state وَحْدَهُaloneشَهِيدwitness جَاءَhe cameكَفَىٰit sufficed
Before you close the book

"They are not difficult — they are unfamiliar. And the unfamiliar becomes familiar through repetition."

That sentence is true of the isnād, and it is true of every remaining obstacle between you and this language. Nothing left is hard. Things are only new — and newness has a cure, and the cure is time.

End of Chapter 23Next → The Register of the Lecture
Day 24 · Register: the lecture

The Register
of the Lecture

A lecture is not a list of facts — it is an argument, held together by a small set of signposts. اِعْلَمْ opens, أَيْ explains, ثُمَّ sequences, لٰكِنْ turns, إِذَنْ concludes. Learn the signposts and you can follow the shape of a talk long before you know all its words.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Recognise the discourse markers that structure a lecture — the words that announce “a reason,” “an example,” “a conclusion.”
  2. Use the shaykh's attention verbs: اِعْلَمْ (know!), اُنْظُرْ (look), تَأَمَّلْ (ponder) — and the frame اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ (know that...).
  3. Follow an argument by its joints alone, even while the nouns are still unfamiliar.
  4. Tell apart بَلْ (rather) and لٰكِنَّ (but) — two “turns” that do different work.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech — teal is rafʿ, blue is naṣb, purple is jarr, red is the verb, grey is a frozen particle. In this chapter, notice how many of the new words are grey: a discourse marker is a signpost, and signposts do not decline.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01اِعْلَمْ
know! (be aware that)
verb · commandع – ل – مframe: اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ
اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ الْعِلْمَ عِبَادَةٌ.

Know that knowledge is worship.

The classic opener of a thousand classical books: اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ... The teacher plants a flag — what follows matters.

02أَيْ
that is; i.e.
particle · explanatoryrenames the word before it
الْفُصْحَىٰ، أَيِ الْعَرَبِيَّةُ الْفَصِيحَةُ.

al-fuṣḥā — that is, clear Arabic.

Introduces a restatement. The noun after أَيْ renames the noun before it, and takes the same case.

03يَعْنِي
it means; that is
verb · Form I · defectiveع – ن – ي
الْبَلَاغُ، يَعْنِي التَّبْلِيغَ.

al-balāgh — meaning, conveyance.

The spoken cousin of أَيْ. You will hear يَعْنِي in every recorded lecture — sometimes carrying meaning, sometimes pure filler. Its noun is مَعْنًى, a meaning.

04الْمَقْصُودُ
what is meant; the point
done-to nounق – ص – د
الْمَقْصُودُ مِنَ الْآيَةِ وَاضِحٌ.

The point of the verse is clear.

Literally “the intended” — the done-to noun of قَصَدَ (Section 7). When a shaykh says وَالْمَقْصُودُ..., the summary is arriving.

05مَثَلًا
for example
adverb · always naṣbم – ث – ل
خُذْ مَثَلًا سُورَةَ الْفَاتِحَةِ.

Take, for example, Sūrat al-Fātiḥah.

Frozen in naṣb — hear the -an and you know an example is coming. Its cousin مِثْل means “like.”

06ثُمَّ
then; thereafter
particle · conjunctionsequence with a gap
تَعَلَّمِ الْحُرُوفَ، ثُمَّ الْكَلِمَاتِ.

Learn the letters, then the words.

ثُمَّ is sequence with a pause — one thing, then later the next. Contrast فَـ, which is immediate.

07بَلْ
rather; on the contrary
particle · retractionالإِضْرَاب
لَيْسَ صَعْبًا، بَلْ غَرِيبٌ.

It is not difficult — rather, it is unfamiliar.

بَلْ cancels what came before and replaces it. Stronger than “but”: it is a correction, not a qualification.

08لٰكِنَّ
but; however
particle · a sister of إِنَّgoverns: naṣb on its noun
الطَّرِيقُ طَوِيلٌ، لٰكِنَّهُ وَاضِحٌ.

The road is long, but it is clear.

The heavy لٰكِنَّ governs like إِنَّ — naṣb on its noun, rafʿ on its news. Its light twin لٰكِنْ governs nothing.

09إِذَنْ
therefore; in that case
particle · inferenceties the knot
إِذَنْ، الْمَعْنَىٰ ظَاهِرٌ.

Therefore, the meaning is evident.

Draws the conclusion. When you hear إِذَنْ, the shaykh is about to tie the argument together.

10بِنَاءً عَلَىٰ
based on; accordingly
phrase · بِنَاءً naṣbب – ن – ي
بِنَاءً عَلَىٰ هٰذَا، لَا يَجُوزُ.

Based on this, it is not permitted.

بِنَاءً is a مَفْعُول لِأَجْلِهِ in naṣb — “building upon.” The ruling rests on what came just before.

11مِنْ هُنَا
from here → hence
phraselinks a ruling to its reason
مِنْ هُنَا قَالَ الْعُلَمَاءُ بِالْوُجُوبِ.

Hence the scholars held it obligatory.

Signals that what follows is a consequence of the point just made. A favourite hinge in fiqh and tafsīr.

12الْخُلَاصَةُ
the summary; in sum
nounخ – ل – ص
الْخُلَاصَةُ: النِّيَّةُ شَرْطٌ.

In sum: intention is a condition.

From خَلَصَ, to be pure — the summary is the matter with everything extra boiled off. When a shaykh reaches الْخُلَاصَةُ, the verdict follows.

13نَنْتَقِلُ
we move on
verb · Form VIIIن – ق – ل
نَنْتَقِلُ إِلَى الْمَسْأَلَةِ الثَّانِيَةِ.

We move on to the second issue.

The transition verb (Section 7). It, and its command اِنْتَقِلْ, mark the seam between two topics.

14تَأَمَّلْ
ponder; reflect closely
verb · Form V · commandأ – م – ل
تَأَمَّلْ قَوْلَهُ تَعَالَىٰ.

Ponder His saying, Most High.

Stronger than “look.” An invitation to stop and turn the meaning over. The attention verbs — اُنْظُرْ · تَأَمَّلْ · اِعْلَمْ — are a shaykh's way of pointing.

15عَلَىٰ كُلِّ حَالٍ
in any case; at any rate
phrasesets a digression aside
عَلَىٰ كُلِّ حَالٍ، الْأَمْرُ سَهْلٌ.

In any case, the matter is easy.

The shaykh closing a digression and returning to the main road. Grammatically a جَارّ وَمَجْرُور standing outside the sentence.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ.

Know that knowledge is light.

The commonest opening in the classical tradition. اِعْلَمْ plants a flag; أَنَّ + a sentence carries what it wants you to know.

Iʿrāb — word by word اِعْلَمْknow! — a command (the attention-opener) · فِعْل أَمْر مَبْنِيّ— أَنَّthat — a sister of inna · حَرْف تَوْكِيد وَنَصْب— الْعِلْمَthe noun of anna · اِسْم أَنَّمَنْصُوب نُورٌthe news of anna · خَبَر أَنَّمَرْفُوع
02
الْفُصْحَىٰ، أَيِ الْعَرَبِيَّةُ الْفَصِيحَةُ.

al-fuṣḥā — that is, clear Arabic.

The explanatory أَيْ. It renames the noun before it — and the new noun takes the same case, here rafʿ.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْفُصْحَىٰthe topic — an aside will rename it · مُبْتَدَأ، مَرْفُوع بِضَمَّة مُقَدَّرَةمَرْفُوع أَيِthat is — the explanatory particle · حَرْف تَفْسِير— الْعَرَبِيَّةُrenaming al-fuṣḥā — an explanatory apposition · عَطْف بَيَانمَرْفُوع الْفَصِيحَةُits adjective · نَعْتمَرْفُوع
03
لَيْسَ صَعْبًا، بَلْ غَرِيبٌ.

It is not difficult — rather, it is unfamiliar.

The retraction بَلْ. It cancels what precedes and swaps naṣb for rafʿ — the case itself tells you the sentence has turned.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَيْسَis not — a sister of kāna; its noun hidden ‘it’ · فِعْل مَاضٍ نَاقِص— صَعْبًاthe news of laysa · خَبَر لَيْسَمَنْصُوب بَلْrather — a retraction particle · حَرْف إِضْرَاب— غَرِيبٌthe news of a new, understood topic — so rafʿ · خَبَر لِمُبْتَدَأ مَحْذُوفمَرْفُوع
04
ثُمَّ نَنْتَقِلُ إِلَى الْمَسْأَلَةِ الثَّانِيَةِ.

Then we move on to the second issue.

The seam of a lecture. ثُمَّ marks the join — sequence with a pause — and نَنْتَقِلُ names the move.

Iʿrāb — word by word ثُمَّthen — a conjunction of sequence · حَرْف عَطْف— نَنْتَقِلُwe move on — present (Form VIII) · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع إِلَىto — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْمَسْأَلَةِobject of ilā · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور الثَّانِيَةِits adjective, so also jarr · نَعْتمَجْرُور
05
إِذَنْ، الْمَقْصُودُ ظَاهِرٌ.

Therefore, the point is evident.

The inference إِذَنْ. It ties the knot, and usually stands outside the sentence's grammar — a pure signpost.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِذَنْtherefore — a particle of inference · حَرْف جَوَاب وَجَزَاء— الْمَقْصُودُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع ظَاهِرٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
06
خُذْ مَثَلًا سُورَةَ الْفَاتِحَةِ.

Take, for example, Sūrat al-Fātiḥah.

مَثَلًا never changes. It is frozen in naṣb — hear the -an and you know an example is coming.

Iʿrāb — word by word خُذْtake! — a command (from أَخَذَ) · فِعْل أَمْر مَبْنِيّ— مَثَلًاfor example — a frozen adverb · مَفْعُول مُطْلَقمَنْصُوب سُورَةَthe object — a possessor · مَفْعُول بِه، مُضَافمَنْصُوب الْفَاتِحَةِthe possessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
07
الطَّرِيقُ طَوِيلٌ، لٰكِنَّهُ وَاضِحٌ.

The road is long, but it is clear.

The heavy لٰكِنَّ. It governs like إِنَّ — naṣb on its noun (the attached ـهُ), rafʿ on its news. The light لٰكِنْ governs nothing.

Iʿrāb — word by word الطَّرِيقُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع طَوِيلٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع لٰكِنَّهُbut + it — lākinna and its noun ‘it’ · لٰكِنَّ وَاسْمُهَا— وَاضِحٌthe news of lākinna · خَبَر لٰكِنَّمَرْفُوع
08
بِنَاءً عَلَىٰ هٰذَا، لَا يَجُوزُ.

Based on this, it is not permitted.

بِنَاءً carries the whole inference — “building on what I just said.” It is a specifier of cause, always in naṣb.

Iʿrāb — word by word بِنَاءًbuilding-upon — a specifier of cause · مَفْعُول لِأَجْلِهِمَنْصُوب عَلَىٰupon — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— هٰذَاthis — a demonstrative; object of ʿalā · اِسْم إِشَارَة مَبْنِيّمَجْرُور مَحَلًّا لَاnot — negation of the present · حَرْف نَفْي— يَجُوزُis permitted — present; doer hidden · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع
09
مِنْ هُنَا قَالَ الْعُلَمَاءُ بِالْوُجُوبِ.

Hence the scholars held it obligatory.

قَالَ بِـ is idiom — “to hold the view of.” And مِنْ هُنَا links a ruling back to the reasoning before it.

Iʿrāb — word by word مِنْfrom — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— هُنَاhere — an adverb of place (frozen); ‘from here’ = hence · ظَرْف مَكَان مَبْنِيّمَجْرُور مَحَلًّا قَالَheld — verb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الْعُلَمَاءُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع بِـin / of — preposition (قَالَ بِـ = held the view) · حَرْف جَرّ— الْوُجُوبِobject of the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
10
الْخُلَاصَةُ: النِّيَّةُ شَرْطٌ.

In sum: intention is a condition.

الْخُلَاصَةُ signals the boiled-down verdict. Everything before was the working; this is the answer — itself a small nominal sentence.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْخُلَاصَةُthe summary — a topic; its news is the sentence that follows · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع النِّيَّةُthe topic of the summarising sentence · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع شَرْطٌits news; the small sentence is خبر of الخلاصة · خَبَرمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — The Signposts of an Argument

A lecture is an argument, and arguments have joints

You met this idea first on Day 8, with لِأَنَّ, حَتَّىٰ and أَمَّا. This chapter completes the set. A shaykh's discourse markers fall into five jobs, and once you can name the job, you can follow the argument without catching every word.

JobMarkersWhat it signals
Open / pointاِعْلَمْ · اُنْظُرْ · تَأَمَّلْ“attend — this matters.”
Explainأَيْ · يَعْنِي · الْمَقْصُودُ · مَثَلًاa restatement, or an example, follows.
Sequenceثُمَّ · فَـ · نَنْتَقِلُa new step, or a new topic.
Turnلٰكِنْ · بَلْa qualification, or a correction.
Concludeإِذَنْ · بِنَاءً عَلَىٰ · مِنْ هُنَا · الْخُلَاصَةُthe verdict is arriving.

The frame اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ — “know that...”

The single most common opening in the whole classical literature. It is a command verb, اِعْلَمْ, followed by أَنَّ and a full sentence:

اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ النِّيَّةَ شَرْطٌ.

Know that intention is a condition.

The whole أَنَّ-clause is the object of “know” — a “masdar-clause,” exactly the machinery of Day 16. When you hear اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ, brace for a thesis, and note the naṣb it throws onto the next noun.

Two turns: لٰكِنْ and بَلْ

English uses “but” for both, and blurs them. Arabic keeps them apart.

MarkerDoesExample
لٰكِنْ / لٰكِنَّqualifies — “X is true, however also Y”الطَّرِيقُ طَوِيلٌ لٰكِنَّهُ وَاضِحٌ
بَلْcancels — “not X, rather Y”لَيْسَ صَعْبًا بَلْ غَرِيبٌ
One governs, one does not

The heavy لٰكِنَّ is a sister of إِنَّ: it puts its noun into naṣb and its news into rafʿ. The light لٰكِنْ — with a sukūn — is a bare conjunction and governs nothing. Same meaning, different grammar, and the shadda on the nūn is the only clue in the text.

Follow the joints — the strategy, completed

For months you will lose most of the nouns in a live lecture. That is expected, and it is survivable, because the joints carry the shape of the argument on their own.

اِعْلَمْ — a thesis is coming. أَيْ / يَعْنِي — he is restating, so you get a second chance at it. مَثَلًا — an example, usually easier than the rule. ثُمَّ — a new step. بَلْ — scrap the last thing. إِذَنْ / الْخُلَاصَةُ — the conclusion, the one sentence to catch if you catch nothing else.

Catch the joints, and you know the skeleton of what was argued even on a day when the vocabulary defeats you. The vocabulary arrives later — and it always arrives.

Section 5Reading Practice — A Shaykh Explains a Word

اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ كَلِمَةَ «التَّقْوَىٰ» أَصْلُهَا مِنَ الْوِقَايَةِ، أَيِ الْحِمَايَةُ. فَالتَّقِيُّ يَقِي نَفْسَهُ، يَعْنِي يَحْمِيهَا مِنَ الْمَعَاصِي.

خُذْ مَثَلًا رَجُلًا يَمْشِي فِي طَرِيقٍ فِيهِ شَوْكٌ؛ فَإِنَّهُ يَرْفَعُ ثَوْبَهُ وَيَتَنَبَّهُ. هٰذِهِ هِيَ التَّقْوَىٰ.

وَقَدْ يَظُنُّ بَعْضُ النَّاسِ أَنَّ التَّقْوَىٰ فِي اللِّبَاسِ وَالظَّاهِرِ، لٰكِنَّهَا فِي الْقَلْبِ. بَلْ هِيَ عَمَلُ الْقَلْبِ أَوَّلًا.

إِذَنْ، الْخُلَاصَةُ: التَّقْوَىٰ حِمَايَةٌ، وَمَحَلُّهَا الْقَلْبُ. ثُمَّ نَنْتَقِلُ إِلَى الْكَلِمَةِ الثَّانِيَةِ.

Know that the word taqwā comes originally from wiqāyah — that is, protection. The God-conscious person guards himself; meaning, he protects himself from acts of disobedience.

Take, for example, a man walking on a road with thorns in it: he lifts his garment and takes care. This, precisely, is taqwā.

Some people suppose that taqwā is in the clothing and the outward — but it is in the heart. Rather, it is an act of the heart first of all.

Therefore, in sum: taqwā is protection, and its seat is the heart. Then we move on to the second word.

New: الْوِقَايَة protection · يَقِي he guards · يَحْمِي he protects · شَوْك thorns · يَتَنَبَّه he takes heed · مَحَلّ seat, place

Count the signposts

In four short paragraphs the shaykh used اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ · أَيْ · يَعْنِي · مَثَلًا · لٰكِنَّ · بَلْ · إِذَنْ · الْخُلَاصَةُ · ثُمَّ · نَنْتَقِلُ — every marker in today's list, in one natural stretch of teaching. Read it again and watch only the grey words: they alone tell you it is a definition, then an example, then a correction, then a conclusion.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

اِعْلَمُوا أَنَّ فَهْمَ الدَّرْسِ لَا يَكُونُ بِكَثْرَةِ الْكَلَامِ، بَلْ بِالتَّأَمُّلِ.

تَأَمَّلُوا كُلَّ كَلِمَةٍ، ثُمَّ اسْأَلُوا. أَمَّا مَنْ يَسْأَلُ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَتَأَمَّلَ، فَإِنَّهُ يَضِيعُ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، يَعْنِي نَسْكُتُ دَائِمًا؟

الشَّيْخُ:

لَا، بَلِ اسْأَلْ. لٰكِنِ اسْأَلْ بَعْدَ التَّأَمُّلِ. عَلَىٰ كُلِّ حَالٍ، السُّؤَالُ الْجَيِّدُ نِصْفُ الْعِلْمِ.

Shaykh: Know that understanding the lesson does not come by much talking — rather, by reflection. Ponder every word, then ask. As for the one who asks before he reflects, he goes astray.

Student: Shaykh, meaning — should we always keep silent?

Shaykh: No; rather, ask. But ask after reflection. In any case, a good question is half of knowledge.

New: كَثْرَة abundance · يَضِيعُ he is lost · نَسْكُتُ we keep silent · الْجَيِّد the good one

Say it yourself

The signposts of a lecture, in your own voice.

اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ الصَّبْرَ مِفْتَاحُ الْفَرَجِ.

لَيْسَ صَعْبًا، بَلْ يَحْتَاجُ إِلَىٰ صَبْرٍ.

إِذَنْ، نَنْتَقِلُ إِلَى الْمَسْأَلَةِ الثَّانِيَةِ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verbقَصَدَ
maṣdarقَصْدaim, intent
to intend, to mean
Form I · sound · يَقْصِدُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIقَصَدْتُأَقْصِدُ——
نَحْنُweقَصَدْنَانَقْصِدُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)قَصَدْتَتَقْصِدُاِقْصِدْلَا تَقْصِدْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)قَصَدْتِتَقْصِدِينَاِقْصِدِيلَا تَقْصِدِي
هُوَheقَصَدَيَقْصِدُ——
هِيَsheقَصَدَتْتَقْصِدُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)قَصَدْتُمَاتَقْصِدَانِاِقْصِدَالَا تَقْصِدَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)قَصَدْتُمْتَقْصِدُونَاِقْصِدُوالَا تَقْصِدُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)قَصَدْتُمَاتَقْصِدَانِاِقْصِدَالَا تَقْصِدَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)قَصَدْتُنَّتَقْصِدْنَاِقْصِدْنَلَا تَقْصِدْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)قَصَدَايَقْصِدَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)قَصَدُوايَقْصِدُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)قَصَدَتَاتَقْصِدَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)قَصَدْنَيَقْصِدْنَ——

The verb behind vocab word 4. Its done-to noun, مَقْصُود, is the point — literally “the intended.” When a shaykh says وَالْمَقْصُودُ..., the conclusion is coming.

verbاِنْتَقَلَ
maṣdarاِنْتِقَالmoving, transition
to move on, to shift
Form VIII · يَنْتَقِلُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIاِنْتَقَلْتُأَنْتَقِلُ——
نَحْنُweاِنْتَقَلْنَانَنْتَقِلُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)اِنْتَقَلْتَتَنْتَقِلُاِنْتَقِلْلَا تَنْتَقِلْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)اِنْتَقَلْتِتَنْتَقِلِينَاِنْتَقِلِيلَا تَنْتَقِلِي
هُوَheاِنْتَقَلَيَنْتَقِلُ——
هِيَsheاِنْتَقَلَتْتَنْتَقِلُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)اِنْتَقَلْتُمَاتَنْتَقِلَانِاِنْتَقِلَالَا تَنْتَقِلَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)اِنْتَقَلْتُمْتَنْتَقِلُونَاِنْتَقِلُوالَا تَنْتَقِلُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)اِنْتَقَلْتُمَاتَنْتَقِلَانِاِنْتَقِلَالَا تَنْتَقِلَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)اِنْتَقَلْتُنَّتَنْتَقِلْنَاِنْتَقِلْنَلَا تَنْتَقِلْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)اِنْتَقَلَايَنْتَقِلَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)اِنْتَقَلُوايَنْتَقِلُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)اِنْتَقَلَتَاتَنْتَقِلَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)اِنْتَقَلْنَيَنْتَقِلْنَ——

The transition verb. نَنْتَقِلُ إِلَىٰ... — “we move on to...” — marks the seam between two topics. The same root ن ق ل gives نَقْل, transmission.

verbتَأَمَّلَ
maṣdarتَأَمُّلcontemplation
to ponder, to reflect closely
Form V · يَتَأَمَّلُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIتَأَمَّلْتُأَتَأَمَّلُ——
نَحْنُweتَأَمَّلْنَانَتَأَمَّلُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)تَأَمَّلْتَتَتَأَمَّلُتَأَمَّلْلَا تَتَأَمَّلْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)تَأَمَّلْتِتَتَأَمَّلِينَتَأَمَّلِيلَا تَتَأَمَّلِي
هُوَheتَأَمَّلَيَتَأَمَّلُ——
هِيَsheتَأَمَّلَتْتَتَأَمَّلُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)تَأَمَّلْتُمَاتَتَأَمَّلَانِتَأَمَّلَالَا تَتَأَمَّلَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)تَأَمَّلْتُمْتَتَأَمَّلُونَتَأَمَّلُوالَا تَتَأَمَّلُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)تَأَمَّلْتُمَاتَتَأَمَّلَانِتَأَمَّلَالَا تَتَأَمَّلَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)تَأَمَّلْتُنَّتَتَأَمَّلْنَتَأَمَّلْنَلَا تَتَأَمَّلْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)تَأَمَّلَايَتَأَمَّلَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)تَأَمَّلُوايَتَأَمَّلُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)تَأَمَّلَتَاتَتَأَمَّلَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)تَأَمَّلْنَيَتَأَمَّلْنَ——

Stronger than “look.” تَأَمَّلْ asks you to stop and turn a meaning over. A Form V verb — the doer acts upon himself — so it takes no passive; that empty column is the signature of the mould.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to intendقَصَدَيَقْصِدُقَصْدقَاصِدقُصِدَيُقْصَدُمَقْصُوداِقْصِدْ
to move on (VIII)اِنْتَقَلَيَنْتَقِلُاِنْتِقَالمُنْتَقِل———اِنْتَقِلْ
to ponder (V)تَأَمَّلَيَتَأَمَّلُتَأَمُّلمُتَأَمِّل———تَأَمَّلْ
One root through all eight moulds — قَصَدَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ق ص د, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. قَصَدَ الشَّيْخُ مَعْنًى دَقِيقًا. — The shaykh intended a subtle meaning.

المضارع · present active. مَاذَا يَقْصِدُ الْعَالِمُ بِهٰذَا؟ — What does the scholar mean by this?

المصدر · the action itself. النِّيَّةُ هِيَ الْقَصْدُ. — Intention is, precisely, the aim.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْقَاصِدُ إِلَىٰ بَيْتِ اللهِ حَاجٌّ. — The one heading for the House of Allah is a pilgrim.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. مَاذَا قُصِدَ بِهٰذِهِ الْآيَةِ؟ — What was meant by this verse?

المضارع المجهول · present passive. لَا يُقْصَدُ اللَّفْظُ، بَلِ الْمَعْنَىٰ. — The wording is not what is meant — rather, the meaning is.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. الْمَقْصُودُ مِنَ الْكَلَامِ وَاضِحٌ. — The point of the speech is clear.

الأمر · command. اِقْصِدْ وَجْهَ اللهِ بِعَمَلِكَ. — Intend the face of Allah with your deed.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

اِعْلَمْknow!أَيْthat is; i.e. يَعْنِيit meansالْمَقْصُودُthe point مَثَلًاfor exampleثُمَّthen بَلْratherلٰكِنَّbut, however إِذَنْthereforeبِنَاءً عَلَىٰbased on مِنْ هُنَاhenceالْخُلَاصَةُthe summary نَنْتَقِلُwe move onتَأَمَّلْponder عَلَىٰ كُلِّ حَالٍin any case

Spiral recap — Chapters 8, 15, 16 & 19

لِأَنَّbecauseأَمَّاas for حَتَّىٰuntil; so thatإِنَّمَاonly أَنْto, thatكَيْso that إِذَاwhen, ifلَوْلَاwere it not for
Before you close the book

You have just learned the words that carry a lecture — not its content, but its shape.

From here on, when a shaykh speaks, listen first for the joints: اِعْلَمْ that something matters, أَيْ that he is restating, ثُمَّ that he has moved on, لٰكِنْ that he is turning, إِذَنْ that he is concluding. You will lose the nouns and keep the argument — and the nouns come later, as they always do.

End of Chapter 24Next → Fiqh & Tafsīr Vocabulary
Day 25 · Register: fiqh & tafsīr

The Words
of the Ruling

Open any book of fiqh and the same few dozen words recur on every page: a ruling, its evidence, and the source it comes from. Learn this closed set once and the technical literature — long feared — becomes navigable. This chapter is vocabulary-heavy on purpose: it is the key to the room.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Know the five rulings — وَاجِب · مُسْتَحَبّ · مُبَاح · مَكْرُوه · حَرَام — and how each is stated.
  2. Name the four sources — الْكِتَاب · السُّنَّة · الْإِجْمَاع · الْقِيَاس — the backbone of every fiqh argument.
  3. Read the core tafsir vocabulary: التَّفْسِير · سَبَب النُّزُول · النَّاسِخ وَالْمَنْسُوخ.
  4. See how the doer / done-to distinction from the derivation tables quietly runs the whole legal vocabulary.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech — teal is rafʿ, blue is naṣb, purple is jarr, red is the verb, grey is a frozen particle. Fiqh sentences lean heavily on iḍāfa — watch the purple cascade through the chains of possession.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01الْحُكْمُ
the ruling, the verdict
nounح – ك – مplural الْأَحْكَام
الْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ.

The ruling belongs to Allah.

In fiqh, the verdict on an act — one of the five categories below. Its plural الْأَحْكَام names the whole subject matter of the law.

02وَاجِب
obligatory
doer-noun · فاعلو – ج – ب= فَرْض
الصَّلَاةُ وَاجِبَةٌ.

Prayer is obligatory.

“That which is binding.” Doing it earns reward; leaving it is sin. Note it is an ism fāʿil — the act itself is “doing” the binding.

03مُسْتَحَبّ
recommended
done-to noun of Xح – ب – ب= مَنْدُوب · سُنَّة
صَلَاةُ الضُّحَىٰ مُسْتَحَبَّةٌ.

The Ḍuḥā prayer is recommended.

Rewarded if done, no sin if left. Literally “deemed lovable” — the done-to noun of اِسْتَحَبَّ, to find beloved.

04مُبَاح
permissible (neutral)
done-to nounب – و – ح= جَائِز
الْأَكْلُ وَالشُّرْبُ مُبَاحٌ.

Eating and drinking are permitted.

Neither rewarded nor punished — the neutral middle of the five. The default of things is permission: الْأَصْلُ فِي الْأَشْيَاءِ الْإِبَاحَةُ.

05مَكْرُوه
disliked
done-to nounك – ر – ه
الْإِسْرَافُ مَكْرُوهٌ.

Extravagance is disliked.

Leaving it is rewarded; doing it is not punished. The done-to noun of كَرِهَ, to dislike — “that which is disliked” [by the Lawgiver].

06حَرَام
forbidden
noun / adjectiveح – ر – مopposite حَلَال
الرِّبَا حَرَامٌ.

Usury is forbidden.

The strongest ruling: doing it is sin, leaving it reward. Its opposite is حَلَال, lawful.

07الدَّلِيلُ
the evidence, the proof
nounد – ل – لplural الْأَدِلَّة
مَا الدَّلِيلُ؟

What is the evidence?

The engine of fiqh. No ruling exists without a دَلِيل — and every scholarly disagreement is, at root, about which evidence applies and how it points.

08الْكِتَابُ
the Book (the Qur'an)
nounك – ت – ب1st of the sources
الْكِتَابُ أَوَّلُ الْأَدِلَّةِ.

The Book is the first of the sources.

As a source of law, الْكِتَاب means the Qur'an specifically. The four sources, in order: الْكِتَاب · السُّنَّة · الْإِجْمَاع · الْقِيَاس.

09السُّنَّةُ
the Sunnah
nounس – ن – ن2nd source
هٰذَا ثَابِتٌ بِالسُّنَّةِ.

This is established by the Sunnah.

The Prophet's ﷺ words, deeds, and tacit approvals — the second source, which explains and details the Book.

10الْإِجْمَاعُ
consensus
noun · maṣdar of IVج – م – ع3rd source
انْعَقَدَ الْإِجْمَاعُ عَلَىٰ ذٰلِكَ.

Consensus was reached on that.

The agreement of the qualified scholars of an age — the third source. From أَجْمَعَ, to resolve upon a thing unanimously.

11الْقِيَاسُ
analogy
noun · maṣdarق – ي – س4th source
الْقِيَاسُ رَابِعُ الْأَدِلَّةِ.

Analogy is the fourth source.

Extending a known ruling to a new case that shares its underlying cause, its عِلَّة. From قَاسَ, to measure one thing against another.

12التَّفْسِيرُ
exegesis, interpretation
noun · maṣdar of IIف – س – ر
عِلْمُ التَّفْسِيرِ شَرِيفٌ.

The science of exegesis is a noble one.

Uncovering the meaning of the Qur'an. Its scholar is a مُفَسِّر; the deeper, interpretive kind is called تَأْوِيل.

13سَبَبُ النُّزُولِ
the occasion of revelation
phrase · iḍāfaن – ز – ل
لِهٰذِهِ الْآيَةِ سَبَبُ نُزُولٍ.

This verse has an occasion of revelation.

The event a verse came down in response to — a key tool of tafsir, because knowing the occasion often fixes the meaning.

14النَّسْخُ
abrogation
noun · maṣdarن – س – خ
فِي الْقُرْآنِ نَاسِخٌ وَمَنْسُوخٌ.

In the Qur'an there is abrogating and abrogated.

A later ruling lifting an earlier one. The pair is a perfect doer/done-to: نَاسِخ the abrogating, مَنْسُوخ the abrogated.

15الْمَذْهَبُ
the school of law
nounذ – ه – بplural الْمَذَاهِب
الْمَذَاهِبُ الْأَرْبَعَةُ مَشْهُورَةٌ.

The four schools are well known.

Literally “a way gone down” — a systematic body of fiqh built on one imam's method: Ḥanafī, Mālikī, Shāfiʿī, Ḥanbalī.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
الْحُكْمُ الشَّرْعِيُّ يَحْتَاجُ إِلَىٰ دَلِيلٍ.

A legal ruling requires an evidence.

The first principle of fiqh. No حُكْم stands without a دَلِيل behind it.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْحُكْمُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع الشَّرْعِيُّits adjective — “legal, of the Sharīʿah” · نَعْتمَرْفُوع يَحْتَاجُrequires — present; the sentence is the news · فِعْل مُضَارِع، وَالجُمْلَة خَبَرمَرْفُوع إِلَىٰto — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— دَلِيلٍobject of ilā · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
02
الصَّلَاةُ وَاجِبَةٌ بِـالْكِتَابِ وَالسُّنَّةِ.

Prayer is obligatory by the Book and the Sunnah.

Two sources in one verdict. وَاجِبَة is an ism fāʿil — the act itself “does” the binding.

Iʿrāb — word by word الصَّلَاةُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع وَاجِبَةٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع بِـby — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْكِتَابِobject of bi- · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور وَand — conjunction · حَرْف عَطْف— السُّنَّةِjoined on, so also jarr · مَعْطُوفمَجْرُور
03
الْخَمْرُ حَرَامٌ بِـنَصِّ الْقُرْآنِ.

Wine is forbidden by the text of the Qur'an.

نَصّ = an explicit text. And حَرَام is the strongest of the five rulings.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْخَمْرُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع حَرَامٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع بِـby — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— نَصِّobject of bi- — a possessor · اِسْم مَجْرُور، مُضَافمَجْرُور الْقُرْآنِthe possessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
04
أَجْمَعَ الْعُلَمَاءُ عَلَىٰ هٰذَا الْحُكْمِ.

The scholars reached consensus on this ruling.

أَجْمَعَ عَلَىٰ — “to agree upon.” Its maṣdar إِجْمَاع is the third source of law.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَجْمَعَreached consensus — Form IV past · فِعْل مَاضٍ (أَفْعَلَ)— الْعُلَمَاءُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع عَلَىٰupon — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— هٰذَاthis — a demonstrative; object of ʿalā · اِسْم إِشَارَة مَبْنِيّمَجْرُور مَحَلًّا الْحُكْمِa substitute for the demonstrative · بَدَلمَجْرُور
05
قِيسَ النَّبِيذُ عَلَى الْخَمْرِ.

Nabīdh was analogized to wine.

Analogy in action. Note قِيسَ — the hollow passive of قَاسَ, exactly like قِيلَ from قَالَ (Day 17).

Iʿrāb — word by word قِيسَwas analogized — a hollow passive verb · فِعْل مَاضٍ مَبْنِيّ لِلْمَجْهُول— النَّبِيذُstands in for the doer · نَائِب فَاعِلمَرْفُوع عَلَىto / upon — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْخَمْرِobject of ʿalā · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
06
مَا سَبَبُ نُزُولِ هٰذِهِ الْآيَةِ؟

What is the occasion of revelation of this verse?

Three iḍāfas in a row — سَبَبُ نُزُولِ الآيَةِ — a signature phrase of tafsir. Each noun possesses the next.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَاwhat — an interrogative; the fronted news · اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَام، خَبَر مُقَدَّممَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا سَبَبُthe occasion — the delayed topic; a possessor · مُبْتَدَأ مُؤَخَّر، مُضَافمَرْفُوع نُزُولِthe possessed — itself a possessor · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِ، مُضَافمَجْرُور هٰذِهِthis — a demonstrative in the iḍāfa · اِسْم إِشَارَة مَبْنِيّمَجْرُور مَحَلًّا الْآيَةِthe possessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِ / بَدَلمَجْرُور
07
هٰذِهِ الْآيَةُ نَاسِخَةٌ لِـمَا قَبْلَهَا.

This verse abrogates what is before it.

The abrogating and the abrogated. نَاسِخ (doer) and مَنْسُوخ (done-to) — a fāʿil/mafʿūl pair doing the work of tafsir.

Iʿrāb — word by word هٰذِهِthis — the topic · مُبْتَدَأ مَبْنِيّمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا الْآيَةُa substitute for the demonstrative · بَدَلمَرْفُوع نَاسِخَةٌthe news — a doer-noun · خَبَر، اِسْم فَاعِلمَرْفُوع لِـfor — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— مَاwhat — a relative noun; object of the lām · اِسْم مَوْصُول مَبْنِيّمَجْرُور مَحَلًّا قَبْلَهَاbefore it — an adverb (the ṣila) · ظَرْف + هَا مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَنْصُوب
08
فَسَّرَ ابْنُ عَبَّاسٍ هٰذِهِ الْكَلِمَةَ.

Ibn ʿAbbās interpreted this word.

تَفْسِير = to uncover a meaning. Ibn ʿAbbās رضي الله عنه is titled تُرْجُمَان الْقُرْآن — the interpreter of the Qur'an.

Iʿrāb — word by word فَسَّرَinterpreted — Form II past · فِعْل مَاضٍ (فَعَّلَ)— ابْنُthe doer — a possessor · فَاعِل، مُضَافمَرْفُوع عَبَّاسٍthe possessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور هٰذِهِthis — the object · اِسْم إِشَارَة مَبْنِيّمَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا الْكَلِمَةَa substitute for the demonstrative · بَدَلمَنْصُوب
09
يَجُوزُ الْجَمْعُ بَيْنَ الصَّلَاتَيْنِ فِي السَّفَرِ.

Combining the two prayers is permitted while travelling.

يَجُوزُ — the impersonal verb of a permitted (mubāḥ) act. And الصَّلَاتَيْنِ is a dual, showing jarr with a ي.

Iʿrāb — word by word يَجُوزُis permitted — present · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع الْجَمْعُcombining — the doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع بَيْنَbetween — an adverb; a possessor · ظَرْف مَكَان، مُضَافمَنْصُوب الصَّلَاتَيْنِthe two prayers — a dual, jarr with a yā · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور فِيin / during — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— السَّفَرِobject of fī · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
10
الْمَذَاهِبُ الْأَرْبَعَةُ كُلُّهَا حَقٌّ.

The four schools — all of them are valid.

كُلُّهَا is a grammatical emphasiser (tawkīd) — it doubles back on “the schools” to close off any exception.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْمَذَاهِبُthe topic (a broken plural) · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع الْأَرْبَعَةُits adjective — a number after its noun · نَعْتمَرْفُوع كُلُّهَاall of them — an emphasiser + ‘them’ · تَوْكِيد + هَا مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَرْفُوع حَقٌّthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — How a Ruling Is Stated

The impersonal verbs of the law

Fiqh has a small set of verbs that state a verdict directly. They are almost always in the present tense, and their subject is the act itself:

VerbStatesExample
يَجِبُit is obligatoryيَجِبُ الصَّوْمُ — fasting is obligatory
يُسْتَحَبُّit is recommendedيُسْتَحَبُّ الذِّكْرُ
يَجُوزُit is permittedيَجُوزُ الْبَيْعُ
يُكْرَهُit is dislikedيُكْرَهُ الْإِسْرَافُ
يَحْرُمُit is forbiddenيَحْرُمُ الرِّبَا

Notice that two of them — يُسْتَحَبُّ and يُكْرَهُ — are passive: “it is deemed recommended,” “it is held disliked.” The passive quietly names the Lawgiver without stating Him, exactly the move you saw in Day 17.

The five rulings are participles — and the mould tells you which

Look again at the five names, and sort them by mould:

RulingMouldReading
وَاجِبdoer-noun · فَاعِلthe act “does” the binding
مُسْتَحَبّ · مَكْرُوه · مُبَاح · مُحَرَّمdone-to noun · مَفْعُولthe act is deemed recommended / disliked / permitted / forbidden

The very same doer/done-to split you drilled in every derivation table is what separates one ruling from another. Grammar is not a preliminary to fiqh; it is the fiqh, worn on the surface of the word.

A pair to lock in: نَاسِخ and مَنْسُوخ

نَاسِخ — the doer: the verse that abrogates. مَنْسُوخ — the done-to: the verse that is abrogated. One root, ن س خ, two moulds, and the whole topic of abrogation rides on telling them apart. If you can hear فَاعِل versus مَفْعُول, you already know which verse is doing the lifting.

Read a fiqh sentence by its ruling-word

A sentence of fiqh has a predictable shape: an act, a ruling on it, and the evidence for that ruling. Find the ruling-word first — وَاجِب, حَرَام, يَجُوزُ — and you have the verdict before you have parsed anything else. Then look for بِـ or لِـ: what follows is almost always the دَلِيل.

The rest — the chains of names, the qualifications, the disagreements — is detail. The skeleton is always: act, ruling, evidence.

Section 5Reading Practice — The Five Rulings

اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ أَفْعَالَ الْمُكَلَّفِ خَمْسَةٌ. فَالصَّلَاةُ الْمَفْرُوضَةُ وَاجِبَةٌ، دَلِيلُهَا الْكِتَابُ وَالسُّنَّةُ. وَصَلَاةُ الضُّحَىٰ مُسْتَحَبَّةٌ، مَنْ فَعَلَهَا أُجِرَ، وَمَنْ تَرَكَهَا فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ.

وَأَمَّا الْأَكْلُ وَالنَّوْمُ فَـمُبَاحٌ، لَا ثَوَابَ فِيهِ وَلَا عِقَابَ. وَالْإِسْرَافُ مَكْرُوهٌ. وَالرِّبَا حَرَامٌ، بِنَصِّ الْقُرْآنِ وَالْإِجْمَاعِ.

فَإِذَا عَرَفْتَ الْحُكْمَ، فَاسْأَلْ عَنْ دَلِيلِهِ. مِنْ هُنَا يَبْدَأُ الْفِقْهُ.

Know that the acts of the accountable person are five. The obligatory prayer is wājib — its evidence is the Book and the Sunnah. And the Ḍuḥā prayer is recommended: whoever does it is rewarded, and whoever leaves it, there is no sin upon him.

As for eating and sleeping, they are permitted — no reward in them and no punishment. And extravagance is disliked. And usury is forbidden, by the text of the Qur'an and by consensus.

So once you know the ruling, ask about its evidence. From here, fiqh begins.

New: الْمُكَلَّف the accountable person · الْمَفْرُوضَة the obligatory (prayer) · أُجِرَ he was rewarded · إِثْم sin · ثَوَاب reward · عِقَاب punishment

Count the vocabulary you now own

In three short paragraphs: the five rulings, two of the four sources, دَلِيل, نَصّ, and the discourse markers of Day 24 — اِعْلَمْ أَنَّ · أَمَّا ... فَـ · مِنْ هُنَا — holding it all together. This is a real page of fiqh, and you can read it.

Section 6Listening Practice

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، مَا حُكْمُ صِيَامِ يَوْمِ الْجُمُعَةِ وَحْدَهُ؟

الشَّيْخُ:

مَكْرُوهٌ. وَالدَّلِيلُ حَدِيثٌ فِي الصَّحِيحِ: نَهَىٰ النَّبِيُّ ﷺ أَنْ يُخَصَّ يَوْمُ الْجُمُعَةِ بِصِيَامٍ.

فَإِنْ صَامَ قَبْلَهُ يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْدَهُ، فَلَا كَرَاهَةَ. تَأَمَّلْ كَيْفَ يُبْنَىٰ الْحُكْمُ عَلَى الدَّلِيلِ.

طَالِبٌ:

إِذَنْ، لَيْسَ كُلُّ مَكْرُوهٍ حَرَامًا؟

الشَّيْخُ:

أَحْسَنْتَ. الْمَكْرُوهُ دُونَ الْحَرَامِ. هٰذَا هُوَ الْفِقْهُ: التَّفْرِيقُ بَيْنَ الْمَرَاتِبِ.

Student: Shaykh, what is the ruling on fasting Friday alone?

Shaykh: Disliked. The evidence is a hadith in the Ṣaḥīḥ: the Prophet ﷺ forbade that Friday be singled out for fasting. But if one fasts a day before it or after, there is no dislike. Ponder how the ruling is built upon the evidence.

Student: So then — not everything disliked is forbidden?

Shaykh: Well done. The disliked is below the forbidden. This is fiqh itself: distinguishing the degrees.

New: صِيَام fasting · يُخَصّ it is singled out · كَرَاهَة dislike · دُونَ below, less than · الْمَرَاتِب the degrees

Say it yourself

The language of the ruling, in your own voice.

مَا حُكْمُ هٰذَا؟ وَمَا الدَّلِيلُ؟

هٰذَا وَاجِبٌ بِالْكِتَابِ وَالسُّنَّةِ.

لَيْسَ كُلُّ مَكْرُوهٍ حَرَامًا.

Section 7Word Sheets

verbحَكَمَ
maṣdarحُكْمruling, judgement
to judge, to rule
Form I · sound · يَحْكُمُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIحَكَمْتُأَحْكُمُ——
نَحْنُweحَكَمْنَانَحْكُمُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)حَكَمْتَتَحْكُمُاُحْكُمْلَا تَحْكُمْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)حَكَمْتِتَحْكُمِينَاُحْكُمِيلَا تَحْكُمِي
هُوَheحَكَمَيَحْكُمُ——
هِيَsheحَكَمَتْتَحْكُمُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)حَكَمْتُمَاتَحْكُمَانِاُحْكُمَالَا تَحْكُمَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)حَكَمْتُمْتَحْكُمُونَاُحْكُمُوالَا تَحْكُمُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)حَكَمْتُمَاتَحْكُمَانِاُحْكُمَالَا تَحْكُمَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)حَكَمْتُنَّتَحْكُمْنَاُحْكُمْنَلَا تَحْكُمْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)حَكَمَايَحْكُمَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)حَكَمُوايَحْكُمُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)حَكَمَتَاتَحْكُمَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)حَكَمْنَيَحْكُمْنَ——

The verb of the whole science. حَكَمَ بِـ — “to rule by [an evidence].” Its doer حَاكِم is a judge or ruler; its done-to مَحْكُوم, the one ruled over; its maṣdar حُكْم, the ruling itself.

verbنَسَخَ
maṣdarنَسْخabrogation; copying
to abrogate; to copy
Form I · sound · يَنْسَخُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIنَسَخْتُأَنْسَخُ——
نَحْنُweنَسَخْنَانَنْسَخُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)نَسَخْتَتَنْسَخُاِنْسَخْلَا تَنْسَخْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)نَسَخْتِتَنْسَخِينَاِنْسَخِيلَا تَنْسَخِي
هُوَheنَسَخَيَنْسَخُ——
هِيَsheنَسَخَتْتَنْسَخُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)نَسَخْتُمَاتَنْسَخَانِاِنْسَخَالَا تَنْسَخَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)نَسَخْتُمْتَنْسَخُونَاِنْسَخُوالَا تَنْسَخُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)نَسَخْتُمَاتَنْسَخَانِاِنْسَخَالَا تَنْسَخَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)نَسَخْتُنَّتَنْسَخْنَاِنْسَخْنَلَا تَنْسَخْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)نَسَخَايَنْسَخَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)نَسَخُوايَنْسَخُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)نَسَخَتَاتَنْسَخَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)نَسَخْنَيَنْسَخْنَ——

One root, two lives. In tafsir, نَسَخَ is to abrogate — a later ruling lifting an earlier one; the pair نَاسِخ / مَنْسُوخ is the abrogating and the abrogated. In everyday Arabic the same verb means to copy — hence نُسْخَة, a copy.

verbفَسَّرَ
maṣdarتَفْسِيرexegesis
to interpret, to explain
Form II · يُفَسِّرُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIفَسَّرْتُأُفَسِّرُ——
نَحْنُweفَسَّرْنَانُفَسِّرُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)فَسَّرْتَتُفَسِّرُفَسِّرْلَا تُفَسِّرْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)فَسَّرْتِتُفَسِّرِينَفَسِّرِيلَا تُفَسِّرِي
هُوَheفَسَّرَيُفَسِّرُ——
هِيَsheفَسَّرَتْتُفَسِّرُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)فَسَّرْتُمَاتُفَسِّرَانِفَسِّرَالَا تُفَسِّرَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)فَسَّرْتُمْتُفَسِّرُونَفَسِّرُوالَا تُفَسِّرُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)فَسَّرْتُمَاتُفَسِّرَانِفَسِّرَالَا تُفَسِّرَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)فَسَّرْتُنَّتُفَسِّرْنَفَسِّرْنَلَا تُفَسِّرْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)فَسَّرَايُفَسِّرَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)فَسَّرُوايُفَسِّرُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)فَسَّرَتَاتُفَسِّرَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)فَسَّرْنَيُفَسِّرْنَ——

To uncover a meaning. Its maṣdar تَفْسِير names the entire science of Qur'anic interpretation; its doer مُفَسِّر is an exegete. Ibn ʿAbbās رضي الله عنه is called تُرْجُمَان الْقُرْآن — the interpreter of the Qur'an.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to ruleحَكَمَيَحْكُمُحُكْمحَاكِمحُكِمَيُحْكَمُمَحْكُوماُحْكُمْ
to abrogateنَسَخَيَنْسَخُنَسْخنَاسِخنُسِخَيُنْسَخُمَنْسُوخاِنْسَخْ
to interpret (II)فَسَّرَيُفَسِّرُتَفْسِيرمُفَسِّرفُسِّرَيُفَسَّرُمُفَسَّرفَسِّرْ
One root through all eight moulds — حَكَمَ
