أَنْتَyou (m.)قَدَّمْتَتُقَدِّمُقَدِّمْلَا تُقَدِّمْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)قَدَّمْتِتُقَدِّمِينَقَدِّمِيلَا تُقَدِّمِي
هُوَheقَدَّمَيُقَدِّمُ——
هِيَsheقَدَّمَتْتُقَدِّمُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)قَدَّمْتُمَاتُقَدِّمَانِقَدِّمَالَا تُقَدِّمَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)قَدَّمْتُمْتُقَدِّمُونَقَدِّمُوالَا تُقَدِّمُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)قَدَّمْتُمَاتُقَدِّمَانِقَدِّمَالَا تُقَدِّمَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)قَدَّمْتُنَّتُقَدِّمْنَقَدِّمْنَلَا تُقَدِّمْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)قَدَّمَايُقَدِّمَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)قَدَّمُوايُقَدِّمُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)قَدَّمَتَاتُقَدِّمَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)قَدَّمْنَيُقَدِّمْنَ——

The verb of word order. To bring a word ahead of its natural place — and, in worship, to prioritise. Its maṣdar تَقْدِيم pairs with تَأْخِير (postponement); its done-to مُقَدَّم is “the fronted [word].” When the mufassir sees a مَفْعُول مُقَدَّم, he asks: why?

verbخَشِيَ
maṣdarخَشْيَةreverent fear, awe
to fear (with knowledge), to stand in awe
Form I · defective · يَخْشَىٰ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIخَشِيتُأَخْشَىٰ——
نَحْنُweخَشِينَانَخْشَىٰ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)خَشِيتَتَخْشَىٰاِخْشَلَا تَخْشَ
أَنْتِyou (f.)خَشِيتِتَخْشَيْنَاِخْشَيْلَا تَخْشَيْ
هُوَheخَشِيَيَخْشَىٰ——
هِيَsheخَشِيَتْتَخْشَىٰ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)خَشِيتُمَاتَخْشَيَانِاِخْشَيَالَا تَخْشَيَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)خَشِيتُمْتَخْشَوْنَاِخْشَوْالَا تَخْشَوْا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)خَشِيتُمَاتَخْشَيَانِاِخْشَيَالَا تَخْشَيَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)خَشِيتُنَّتَخْشَيْنَاِخْشَيْنَلَا تَخْشَيْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)خَشِيَايَخْشَيَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)خَشُوايَخْشَوْنَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)خَشِيَتَاتَخْشَيَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)خَشِينَيَخْشَيْنَ——

A defective verb (final ي), like نَسِيَ → يَنْسَىٰ. Not raw fright (خَوْف) but the awe born of knowing the greatness of the One feared — which is why the verse makes the scholars its subject. Its command اِخْشَ drops the final letter to jazm.

verbدَلَّ
maṣdarدَلَالَةsignification, indication
to indicate, to point to, to guide
Form I · geminate · يَدُلُّ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIدَلَلْتُأَدُلُّ——
نَحْنُweدَلَلْنَانَدُلُّ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)دَلَلْتَتَدُلُّدُلَّلَا تَدُلَّ
أَنْتِyou (f.)دَلَلْتِتَدُلِّينَدُلِّيلَا تَدُلِّي
هُوَheدَلَّيَدُلُّ——
هِيَsheدَلَّتْتَدُلُّ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)دَلَلْتُمَاتَدُلَّانِدُلَّالَا تَدُلَّا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)دَلَلْتُمْتَدُلُّونَدُلُّوالَا تَدُلُّوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)دَلَلْتُمَاتَدُلَّانِدُلَّالَا تَدُلَّا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)دَلَلْتُنَّتَدْلُلْنَاُدْلُلْنَلَا تَدْلُلْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)دَلَّايَدُلَّانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)دَلُّوايَدُلُّونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)دَلَّتَاتَدُلَّانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)دَلَلْنَيَدْلُلْنَ——

Your first geminate (doubled) verb. The two identical letters merge before a vowel (دَلَّ · يَدُلُّ) but split apart before a consonant-suffix (دَلَلْتُ · يَدْلُلْنَ). Its maṣdar دَلَالَة is how a word points to a meaning; its done-to مَدْلُول is the thing pointed to.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to put first (II)قَدَّمَيُقَدِّمُتَقْدِيممُقَدِّمقُدِّمَيُقَدَّمُمُقَدَّمقَدِّمْ
to fear (in awe)خَشِيَيَخْشَىٰخَشْيَةخَاشٍخُشِيَيُخْشَىٰمَخْشِيّاِخْشَ
to indicateدَلَّيَدُلُّدَلَالَةدَالّدُلَّيُدَلُّمَدْلُولدُلَّ
One root through all eight moulds — قَدَّمَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ق د م, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. قَدَّمَ الْعَالِمُ الدَّلِيلَ. — The scholar put forward the evidence.

المضارع · present active. لِمَاذَا يُقَدِّمُ اللهُ الْمَفْعُولَ؟ — Why does Allah put the object first?

المصدر · the action itself. فِي الْآيَةِ تَقْدِيمٌ وَتَأْخِيرٌ. — In the verse there is fronting and postponement.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْمُقَدِّمُ لِلْأَهَمِّ حَكِيمٌ. — One who puts the most important first is wise.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. قُدِّمَ لَفْظُ الْجَلَالَةِ لِلتَّعْظِيمِ. — The Name of Allah was fronted for glorification.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. مَا يُقَدَّمُ يُقْصَدُ بِهِ الْحَصْرُ. — What is fronted, restriction is intended by it.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. الْمَفْعُولُ الْمُقَدَّمُ يُفِيدُ الْحَصْرَ. — A fronted object conveys restriction.

الأمر · command. قَدِّمْ رِضَا اللهِ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ. — Put the pleasure of Allah before everything.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

خَشْيَةreverent aweالْعِبَادthe servants الْمُرَادwhat is meantاللَّفْظthe wording الدَّلَالَةsignificationالْحَصْرrestriction التَّقْدِيمfrontingالْمُقَدَّمthe fronted الْقَرِينَةan indicatorالسِّيَاقthe context الْبَلَاغَةeloquenceالنُّكْتَةa subtle point الظَّاهِرthe apparentالْحِكْمَةwisdom التَّأْوِيلinterpretation

Spiral recap — Chapters 7, 16, 24 & 25

عَرَفَhe knewالْمَعْرُوفthe good أَرَادَhe wantedالْمَقْصُودُthe point التَّفْسِيرُexegesisالْآيَةُthe verse الْمَعْنَىٰthe meaningالْعُلَمَاءُthe scholars
Before you close the book

Four unedited texts — two sūras, a hadith, a scholar's page, and a page of tafsir — and not one of them defeated you. That was the whole wager of this book, and it has been paid.

You do not yet know most of the words. You never will know all of them; no one does. But you will never again be stopped by the grammar — and that was the wall. What remains is the open sea of vocabulary, crossed the only way it can be: by listening, and by time. Tomorrow, the last chapter tells you exactly how.

End of Chapter 29Next → The Road Out
Day 30 · The end of Part 1

The Road Out

This is not another lesson. It is the exit, and the map for everything after it. You have reached the end of the grammar; what remains is a different kind of work, done a different way. Read this chapter once now — and again on the day you close the book for real.

01Where you stand

Thirty days ago you could not reliably tell a sentence with a verb from one without. Look at what you can do now.

  • Hear a vowelled sentence and know its shape — nominal or verbal, who did what to whom — by the endings alone.
  • Read fully-vowelled text, and parse it: why this word is rafʿ, why that one naṣb, why the third takes a fatḥa where you expected a kasra.
  • Follow slow, scripted Arabic at the level of gist.
  • Carry roughly 550 words and — this is the real prize — every major grammatical structure of fuṣḥā: the cases, the moulds, the sisters of كَانَ and إِنَّ, the conditional, the passive, the relative clause, iʿrāb by ear.

And now the honest part, the same one you were promised on the first page: you leave Part 1 with the grammar of an intermediate and the vocabulary of a beginner. That is not a failure of the book. It is its design. Grammar is a closed room, and you have now walked its whole floor. Vocabulary is an open sea — and you cross a sea differently than you pace a room.

02Why you now change methods

For thirty days you learned by studying. From here, you learn by listening — the way you learned English. You did not learn English by memorising a dictionary. You crossed a threshold, after which every new word arrived free, in context, from things you actually wanted to understand. Below that threshold, listening is noise. Above it, listening becomes the teacher, and it never sends a bill.

The comprehension threshold

In a narrow domain — and Islamic lectures are narrow — a vocabulary of about 3,000 words gives you roughly 95% coverage of what is said. At that point the unknown 5% is thin enough to guess from context, and you learn it while listening, without stopping. That is the crossing. You already hold ~550 of those 3,000, and every structure needed to slot the rest into place. The whole remaining job is: reach 3,000, by listening.

03Your Day-31 plan

Two habits, most days, forty-five minutes to an hour. That is all. Consistency beats intensity — أَحَبُّ الْعَمَلِ إِلَى اللهِ أَدْوَمُهُ.

Each dayWhatRoughly
Vocabulary20 new words in an Anki deck, frequency-ordered, plus the day's reviews20 min
Listeningone short lecture clip with a transcript, shadowed25–35 min

04Part one of the day — 20 words, in frequency order

Use a spaced-repetition tool — Anki is the standard. It schedules each card to return just as you are about to forget it, so your effort lands only where it is needed.

Two rules make it work:

  • Frequency order. Get a frequency list for your domain — a Qur'anic vocabulary list, or a frequency list built from lecture transcripts — and learn it in order. The most common 3,000 words are 95% of everything said; learning a rare word before a common one is spending effort you will not soon recover. Every word you learn should be one you are about to meet.
  • Mine your listening. When a word stops you twice in the clips you shadow, make it a card. Words caught in context, from something you wanted to understand, stick far harder than words off a list.

Twenty words a day is about 3,000 in five months. Add the 550 you already carry, and you are at the threshold before the year is out.

The one mistake to avoid

Do not chase interesting rare words. Early on, frequency is everything. A boring common word — قَدْ, لَقَدْ, إِنَّمَا, حَتَّىٰ — buys you more comprehension than ten beautiful rare ones. Learn the boring words first. They are the mortar.

05Part two of the day — shadow a transcript

Pick a short, slow, single-speaker clip — two to five minutes — that has a written transcript. Then:

  1. Listen once, no reading. Catch whatever you can. Do not worry about the rest.
  2. Listen again, reading the transcript. Mark every word that stops you.
  3. Mine it. Add the frequent unknowns to Anki; let the rare ones go for now.
  4. Listen a third time and shadow: speak along a beat behind the voice, matching its sound, its rhythm, its stops — even before you catch every word.
  5. Repeat the same clip across several days until it is transparent — until you hear it as speech, not as a puzzle. Then, and only then, move to the next.

Shadowing welds three things together — the sound, the meaning, and the muscle of saying it. It is the shortest road from “I can parse this on paper” to “I understand this by ear.”

Depth beats breadth

Ten clips truly mastered will take you further than a hundred half-heard. Re-listen without shame. The goal is not to have heard much Arabic; it is to have made some Arabic automatic — and automatic only comes from repetition.

06What to read, and what to hear

Your first real book is الْأَرْبَعُونَ النَّوَوِيَّة — al-Nawawī's Forty Hadith. Short, famous, self-contained, and — as Chapter 27 showed you — already half-open to you. Read one hadith at a time, parse it, and let it teach you its words.

For listening, stay in your domain: scripted, single-speaker Islamic lectures with transcripts; slow Qur'anic recitation with the text before you; recorded tafsīr and hadith explanations. Resist wandering into news Arabic or a spoken dialect for now — your 3,000 words live in this register, and scattering your effort only delays the crossing. Breadth comes free, later, once the threshold is behind you.

07The milestones

So you can feel the ground rising under you, here is what each stage unlocks.

WordsWhat opens
~550 — nowParse any vowelled sentence; read short texts slowly; follow the shape of a lecture by its joints.
~1,100 — end of Book 2Read al-Nawawī's Forty with the dictionary only now and then; follow a simple scripted lecture.
~2,000Follow most of a slow lecture in real time; read short classical texts with growing ease.
~3,000 — the crossingPut the materials down. Learn new words from the lectures themselves, from context — the way you learned English from a screen. You are across.

08The three-part arc

You have now finished the first third of a single, deliberate journey.

Part 1 · this book

The grammar frame

Days 1–30. The full grammar skeleton and your first ~550 words — everything needed to parse, even before you know every word.

Complete · 30 of 30 written
Part 2 · Book 2

Grammar completed, real texts

Thirty more lessons: the grammar finished off, ~1,100 words mined from real lecture transcripts, and al-Nawawī's Forty as your first genuine reading text.

Next
Part 3 · The Crossing

Vocabulary & listening

Six to eight months of the routine on this page — 20 words a day, transcript-shadowing — to ~3,000 words, and then the materials come off.

The destination
A last word

You were not bad at Arabic. You were taught it in a language you were still learning, with words like fāʿil and majrūr that were never translated. This time you were taught it in a language you already knew — and it held. Every ending you once feared, you can now name and explain.

The rest of the road is not harder than what you have already walked. It is only longer. And you know the six things it asks, because al-Shāfiʿī named them and you parsed them: keenness, diligence, a teacher, humility, a little provision — and طُولُ زَمَانٍ, length of time. Give it the time, steadily, and the sea gives way.

لَا تَنْتَظِرْ حَتَّىٰ تَفْهَمَ. اِسْتَمِعْ لِكَيْ تَفْهَمَ.

Do not wait until you understand. Listen in order to understand.

End of Part 1 · Days 1–30Now begin the Crossing →

Chapters
Masterclass · Taught in English, at last

Iʿrāb
Why the last letter keeps changing

You did not fail this topic. You were taught it in a language you were still learning, using words like fāʿil and manṣūb that were never translated. Today, nothing goes untranslated.

Section 1The One Idea

Forget everything for sixty seconds and look at English:

SentenceWord usedWhy
He saw the teacher.hehe is doing
The teacher saw him.himhe is receiving
The book is his.hishe is owning

Same person. Three different shapes: he / him / his. English changes the word to show the job.

Arabic does exactly this — but instead of changing the whole word, it changes only the last vowel. And it does it to every noun in the language, not just to pronouns.

The definition, in one line

Iʿrāb (إِعْرَاب) is the system of endings that tells you what job a word is doing in the sentence. The ending is a job title, pronounced out loud.

That is the whole topic. Everything below is just detail.

Section 2The Three Jobs, The Three Endings

Arabic nouns have exactly three states. Not thirty. Three.

StateEndingThe job, in plain English
Rafʿ
رَفْع
"nominative"		 ـُ   -u
ـٌ   -un		 The doer. Who is performing the action, or what the sentence is about.
Naṣb
نَصْب
"accusative"		 ـَ   -a
ـً   -an		 The target and the extras. What the action lands on — plus how, when, why, how much.
Jarr
جَرّ
"genitive"		 ـِ   -i
ـٍ   -in		 The word being pulled. Anything after a preposition, or anything possessed by the word before it.
The doubled version

Each ending has a plain form and a doubled form. The doubled one — tanwīn — simply means "a / an":

الْكِتَابُ = the book  ·  كِتَابٌ = a book

So the ending carries two pieces of information at once: the job and the definiteness. One syllable, two facts. This is why Arabic can be so compact.

One spelling quirk to know now

When an indefinite noun takes naṣb, Arabic writes an extra alif at the end: كِتَابًا — kitāban. That silent alif is nothing more than a chair for the fatḥatayn to sit on. It is not a letter you pronounce.

Section 3One Sentence, All Three

Here is the sentence that makes the whole system click. Read the endings aloud.

كَتَبَ الطَّالِبُ الدَّرْسَ فِي الْكِتَابِ.

The student wrote the lesson in the book.

verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف
WordEndingStateBecause...
الطَّالِبُ-u  ـُrafʿHe is the doer. He did the writing.
الدَّرْسَ-a  ـَnaṣbIt is the target. The writing landed on it.
الْكِتَابِ-i  ـِjarrIt follows the preposition فِي.

Three words, three different final vowels, three different jobs. Nothing else in the sentence tells you who wrote what. The vowels are doing all the work.

Section 4The Proof: Why Arabic Needs This

Now watch what iʿrāb buys you. Take a sentence:

ضَرَبَ زَيْدٌ عَمْرًا.

Zayd hit ʿAmr.

Now scramble the word order — put ʿAmr first:

ضَرَبَ عَمْرًا زَيْدٌ.

Zayd hit ʿAmr. Still. The meaning has not moved an inch.

Sit with this

In English, "the dog bit the man" and "the man bit the dog" are different events — because English encodes meaning in word order. Arabic encodes it in endings. Zayd carries -un (doer) wherever he stands; ʿAmr carries -an (target) wherever he stands.

This is why Arabic poetry, Qur'anic verses and classical prose can rearrange words so freely and still be perfectly clear. The endings are the glue.

And it is why the endings matter to you, the listener. In a formal lecture the speaker pronounces them. Every -u you catch is the speaker telling you: this is my subject, hold onto it. Every -i says: this one is just hanging off a preposition, don't over-weight it. You are being handed a live grammatical commentary, for free, in real time.

Section 5When Do You Use Each? The Decision Tree

Use rafʿ ـُ when the word is:

RoleArabic termExample
the doer of a verbفَاعِل fāʿilقَالَ الْأُسْتَاذُ — the teacher said
the topic of a verbless sentenceمُبْتَدَأ mubtadaʾالْعِلْمُ نُورٌ — knowledge is light
what is said about that topicخَبَر khabar...نُورٌ — both take -u!
Tie-back to Chapter 1

Look again: الْعِلْمُ نُورٌ. Both words end in a ḍamma sound. That matching pair of -u endings is the audible fingerprint of a nominal sentence. Hear two -us in a row with no verb — you are inside a "X is Y" sentence.

Use naṣb ـَ when the word is:

RoleArabic termExample
the object of a verbمَفْعُول بِهِذَكَرَ الْحَقَّ — he mentioned the truth
a "how / in what manner" wordحَال · adverbتَكَلَّمَ كَثِيرًا — he spoke a lot
a "when / where" wordظَرْفقَرَأْتُ الْيَوْمَ — I read today
the reason for an actionمَفْعُول لِأَجْلِهِقَامَ احْتِرَامًا — he stood out of respect

Think of naṣb as Arabic's default bin for everything extra. If a word is not the doer and not chained to a preposition, it is probably wearing a fatḥa.

Use jarr ـِ when the word:

TriggerExample
follows a preposition (فِي، مِنْ، عَنْ، عَلَى، بِ، لِ)تَكَلَّمَ عَنِ الْعِلْمِ — he spoke about knowledge
is the second noun in a possession chain (إِضَافَة)كِتَابُ الطَّالِبِ — the student's book
The iḍāfa, in one breath

كِتَابُ الطَّالِبِ = "the book of the student." The first noun takes whatever ending its job in the sentence requires. The second noun is always jarr. Always. No exceptions. And the first noun never takes الـ, because the second one already makes it definite.

You already met one: عِنْدَ الْعُلَمَاءِ — "in the view of the scholars." Hear that final -i? That is an iḍāfa announcing itself.

Section 6The Two Sentence-Flippers

Two families of words exist for one purpose: to change the endings of the sentence that follows them. Once you know them, half of "why is that ending like that?" is answered.

① كَانَ and its sisters — the khabar gets knocked down to naṣb

الْعِلْمُ نَافِعٌ.

Knowledge is beneficial. (both rafʿ: -u ... -un)

كَانَ الْعِلْمُ نَافِعًا.

Knowledge was beneficial. (subject stays rafʿ; the predicate flips to -an)

Sisters of كان that behave identically: لَيْسَ (is not), أَصْبَحَ (became), مَا زَالَ (still is), صَارَ (became).

This is why Chapter 1's example was لَيْسَ الْأَمْرُ سَهْلًا — sahlan, not sahlun. Now you know exactly who did that to it.

② إِنَّ and its sisters — the mirror image

إِنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ.

Indeed, knowledge is light. (subject drops to -a; predicate stays -un)

Exactly the reverse of كان. Sisters: أَنَّ (that), لٰكِنَّ (but), لَعَلَّ (perhaps), كَأَنَّ (as if).

Why this pays off immediately

Islamic lectures are saturated with إِنَّ. Every time you hear إِنَّ اللهَ — inna Allāha — with that fatḥa, that is not random. It is إِنَّ forcing the noun into naṣb. You have heard this a thousand times. Now you know why.

Section 7The Good News: Many Words Never Change

Here is the mercy in the system. A large slice of the most common Arabic words are mabnī (مَبْنِيّ) — "built solid." They are frozen. They never take iʿrāb endings at all.

هٰذَاهٰذِهِالَّذِيهُوَهِيَ أَنَانَحْنُمَنْمَاكَيْفَ

Demonstratives, relative pronouns, personal pronouns, question words — all frozen. Every past-tense verb is frozen too (قَالَ is always qāla).

So when your teacher parsed هٰذَا and said مَبْنِيٌّ عَلَى السُّكُونِ فِي مَحَلِّ رَفْعٍ — "frozen, but occupying the position of rafʿ" — he meant: this word is doing the doer's job, but it refuses to wear the doer's badge. You still know its job. You just cannot hear it on the ending.

Section 8When the Ending Is a Letter, Not a Vowel

One last complication, and it is a small one. Some word-types show their case with a letter instead of a vowel. Learn to spot the letter and you have learned the case.

TypeRafʿ (doer)Naṣb & Jarr
Dual
"two"		 الْمُسْلِمَانِ
-āni		 الْمُسْلِمَيْنِ
-ayni
Sound masculine plural الْمُسْلِمُونَ
-ūna		 الْمُسْلِمِينَ
-īna
The "five nouns"
father, brother, etc.		 أَبُوهُ
abū-		 أَبَاهُ / أَبِيهِ
abā- / abī-
A free listening upgrade

You now have a rule that decodes hundreds of words: hear -ūna, the plural is the doer. Hear -īna, it is not.

قَالَ الْمُسْلِمُونَ — the Muslims said.  ·  رَأَيْتُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — I saw the Muslims.

Section 9Verbs Have Iʿrāb Too

Only the present-tense verb changes its ending, and it has three states. This is the entire system:

StateEndingTriggerExample
rafʿ — default-u  ـُnothing in front of itيَكْتُبُ — he writes
naṣb-a  ـَأَنْ (to), لَنْ (will not), كَيْ (in order to)لَنْ يَكْتُبَ — he will not write
jazmsukūn  ـْلَمْ (did not), لَا (don't!), إِنْ (if)لَمْ يَكْتُبْ — he did not write

Jazm (جَزْم) is a fourth state that exists only for verbs — nouns never take it. It means "cut short," which is exactly what the sukūn does to the sound.

Instant payoff

لَمْ takes a present-tense verb but produces a past meaning. لَمْ يَقُلْ = "he did not say." Beginners hear the present-tense shape and mistranslate it as "he does not say." The little لَمْ — and the clipped ending — are the tell.

Section 10Parse a Sentence Like a Scholar

Doing iʿrāb on a sentence means naming each word's job and ending. Here is the ritual, done once, slowly.

ذَكَرَ الْأُسْتَاذُ الْحَقَّ فِي دَرْسِ الْيَوْمِ.

The teacher mentioned the truth in today's lesson.

WordJobStateMarked by
ذَكَرَpast verbfrozen (mabnī)—
الْأُسْتَاذُdoer — fāʿilrafʿḍamma ـُ
الْحَقَّobject — mafʿūl bihinaṣbfatḥa ـَ
فِيprepositionfrozen—
دَرْسِobject of the prepositionjarrkasra ـِ
الْيَوْمِ2nd noun of the iḍāfajarrkasra ـِ

Notice دَرْسِ has no الـ and no tanwīn. That is the signature of the first half of an iḍāfa — it borrows its definiteness from الْيَوْمِ.

Your three-question decoder

For any noun you meet, ask in this order:

1. Is it after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it? → jarr, kasra.
2. Is it the one doing the action, or the topic of a verbless sentence? → rafʿ, ḍamma.
3. Neither? → almost certainly naṣb, fatḥa.

Three questions. That is the whole of iʿrāb for a reader.

Section 11Exercises

A. Name the state and say why

For each underlined word, give the state (rafʿ / naṣb / jarr) and the reason.

  1. قَالَ الرَّجُلُ الْحَقَّ. → الرَّجُلُ
  2. قَالَ الرَّجُلُ الْحَقَّ. → الْحَقَّ
  3. تَكَلَّمْنَا عَنِ الْعِلْمِ. → الْعِلْمِ
  4. إِنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ. → الْعِلْمَ
  5. كَانَ الْأَمْرُ مُهِمًّا. → مُهِمًّا
  6. كِتَابُ الطَّالِبِ جَدِيدٌ. → الطَّالِبِ

B. Supply the missing ending

  1. ذَكَرَ الْأُسْتَاذ__ الدَّرْس__ .   (The teacher mentioned the lesson.)
  2. إِنَّ النَّاس__ كَثِيرُونَ.   (Indeed, the people are many.)
  3. لَيْسَ الْأَمْرُ سَهْل__ .   (The matter is not easy.)
  4. تَكَلَّمَ عَنْ شَيْء__ مُهِمّ__ .   (He spoke about an important thing.)

C. Same words, different endings — different meaning?

Translate both. Then say what changed.

  1. رَأَى الْمُسْلِمُونَ الْأُسْتَاذَ.
  2. رَأَى الْأُسْتَاذُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ.

D. Verb states

Which state is the verb in, and what triggered it?

  1. لَنْ يَذْكُرَ هٰذَا.
  2. لَمْ يَذْكُرْ هٰذَا.
  3. يَذْكُرُ هٰذَا كَثِيرًا.
Answer key

A. 1. rafʿ — it is the fāʿil, the doer of "said." · 2. naṣb — it is the object, what was said. · 3. jarr — it follows the preposition عَنْ. · 4. naṣb — إِنَّ forces its noun into naṣb. · 5. naṣb — كَانَ forces its predicate into naṣb. · 6. jarr — second noun of an iḍāfa.

B. 1. الْأُسْتَاذُ الدَّرْسَ · 2. النَّاسَ · 3. سَهْلًا · 4. شَيْءٍ مُهِمٍّ (the adjective follows its noun into jarr)

C. 1. "The Muslims saw the teacher" — الْمُسْلِمُونَ ends in -ūna, so they are the doers. 2. "The teacher saw the Muslims" — now الْمُسْلِمِينَ ends in -īna, so they are the target. Only the endings changed the meaning.

D. 1. naṣb, triggered by لَنْ — "he will not mention this." · 2. jazm, triggered by لَمْ — "he did not mention this" (past meaning, present-tense shape). · 3. rafʿ, nothing in front of it — "he mentions this often."

Section 12How to Actually Absorb This

Iʿrāb is not learned by memorising the table. It is learned by predicting.

  1. Read aloud with endings. Take any fully-vowelled sentence in this book and pronounce every final vowel, even though native speakers often drop them in casual speech. Your mouth teaches your ear.
  2. Play "guess the ending." Before you reach the end of a word in a text, predict its vowel from its job. Check. You will be wrong often at first and rarely within a month.
  3. Hunt for إِنَّ in lectures. Every time you hear it, listen for the fatḥa on the next noun. This single habit will make the system audible to you.
A last word

Your first teacher was not wrong to teach iʿrāb — he was teaching you the load-bearing wall of the entire language. He simply built it in Arabic, on a foundation you did not have yet. You have the foundation now. Three states, three vowels, three questions. That is all it ever was.

Masterclass · Iʿrāb Slot it after Chapter 3 in your study order
